title: auto-sprint: doer.md's blanket issue_type check contradicts the documented structural (has-children) dispatch rule -- causes nondeterministic skip-vs-work outcomes on leaf bug/feature beads
description:
OBSERVED (this sprint run, auto-sprint/eft-service, apra-fleet-eft cycle 1): two doer dispatches, facing the SAME category of bead (a leaf bug-type bead with zero children -- i.e. genuinely workable, not a decomposed grouping parent), made OPPOSITE decisions:

- apra-fleet-eft.6.7 (issue_type=bug, no children): the doer session reasoned past doer.md's literal text, recognized this bead did NOT fall under the "skip non-task" exception since it has no children, implemented the missing regression test, and correctly closed it. Its own notes explicitly flag the ambiguity: "this bead's issue_type is 'bug' (not 'task') and it has no children ... so per the doer contract's Step 1 exception this did not fall under the 'skip non-task beads with children' carve-out ... would flag this bug/task-type distinction to the orchestrator for future bead typing."
- apra-fleet-eft.13 (issue_type=bug, no children -- VERIFIED via `bd show`/`bd list --parent apra-fleet-eft.13` returning zero children, status still open): a different doer dispatch, given a streak of [eft.10, eft.11, eft.12, eft.13], skipped ALL FOUR purely because "issue_type=feature/bug, not task, per `bd show`" -- WITHOUT checking whether any of them actually have children. eft.10/11/12 are legitimately-undecomposed P3 features (correct to skip), but eft.13 is a real, workable P1 bug with a concrete acceptance criterion (the Plan-reviewer's own earlier verdict this same cycle explicitly called it "a leaf bug with a self-contained acceptance criterion requiring a passing regression test") -- it should have been worked, not skipped. It remains open and unclaimed after this cycle.

ROOT CAUSE (confirmed by reading the actual file, not guessed): `~/.claude/agents/doer.md` itself contains the blanket, type-based rule that caused the second (incorrect) outcome:
  - Step 1 (line ~52-53): "If an assigned id turns out not to be `issue_type=task` (e.g. it is a `bug`/`feature`-typed bead with children, assigned to you in error), skip it, note why in your final report, and do not claim or close it."
  - Line ~122: "Do NOT close features or bugs -- only `issue_type=task` beads."
  - Line ~165: "NEVER close type=feature or type=bug issues"

This directly contradicts the system's own documented dispatch-eligibility semantics (runner.js comments, apra-fleet-xbu.C5, referenced multiple times in this session's other fixes): "a bead that has been decomposed into subtasks ... must never be dispatched to a doer alongside its own subtasks ... This is a STRUCTURAL check (does this ready bead have children in scope?), not an issue_type check -- retyping a bead to change its dispatch eligibility does not work ... A bead can be a leaf `type=task` OR a decomposed `type=bug`/`type=feature` parent; only the has-children structure tells them apart."

So the orchestrator side (runner.js's readyLeafBeads()) already implements the CORRECT structural rule, but doer.md's own written instructions to the LLM doer still describe the WRONG, purely type-based rule -- doer.md's parenthetical "(e.g. it is a bug/feature-typed bead with children...)" gestures at the structural distinction but the actual operative sentence ("If an assigned id turns out not to be issue_type=task ... skip it") and the blanket "NEVER close type=feature or type=bug" reinforce the type-only reading. Different doer sessions resolve this ambiguity differently -- one reads past it to the structural intent (correct), another follows the literal, simpler rule (incorrect) -- producing genuinely nondeterministic dispatch outcomes on the exact same category of bead.

SECONDARY EFFECT (orchestrator bookkeeping): when a doer streak entirely consists of skipped non-task beads (correct or not), the orchestrator currently logs it as "reported status VERIFY but bead(s) still open -- treating streak as FAILED." This conflates "doer correctly determined there was nothing to do" with "doer failed to do assigned work" -- both eft.13's genuine miss and any hypothetical correct all-skip streak look identical in the log as a bare "FAILED" outcome, adding noise and making a real miss (like eft.13) harder to notice/triage after the fact.

FIX DIRECTION (not yet implemented, needs doer.md text revision + possibly an orchestrator classification, not attempted here since doer.md is applied fleet-wide and changing it needs care): rewrite doer.md's Step 1/Step 2/Step 3 exception language to state the STRUCTURAL rule explicitly and primarily ("skip an assigned bead only if `bd show`/`bd list --parent <id>` shows it has children -- i.e. it is a decomposed grouping parent, regardless of its issue_type; a childless bug/feature is a real leaf and must be worked like a task"), removing or clarifying the misleading blanket "NEVER close type=feature or type=bug" line so it doesn't contradict the structural exception it's meant to describe. Separately, consider having the orchestrator distinguish "all-skipped, correctly" from "genuinely failed" streak outcomes in its logging/streak-outcome classification, so a real miss doesn't hide inside undifferentiated "FAILED" noise.

title: auto-sprint: dashboard Sprint tree nests by 'blocks' dependency edges, not 'parent' containment -- grandchildren render as flat top-level rows
description:
OBSERVED (per user's concern: "it is extremely important that the tree should look like a tree and not a flat list"): even after the auto-sprint-3 fix (bdListScoped now correctly includes descendants at any depth in sprintTasks), a level-3 grandchild (e.g. apra-fleet-eft.1.2, child of eft.1 via `--parent`) will show up in the dashboard's "Sprint" section instead of "Backlog" -- but it will NOT render nested/indented under eft.1 in the visual tree.

ROOT CAUSE: packages/apra-fleet-se/auto-sprint/viewer-extensions.mjs's renderBeadsHtml() builds the tree entirely from `blocks`-type dependency edges (t.dependencies.filter(d => d.type === 'blocks')), explicitly NOT from `parent` (its own comment at line ~176: "Build a dependency tree from `blocks`-type edges, not `parent`"). A bead is nested under whichever bead its `blocks` dependency points at (its "primary blocker"); a bead with NO `blocks` edge to anything renders as a top-level root row regardless of its `--parent`.

Per this project's own GRAPH-SEMANTICS.md convention (referenced in runner.js comments), `parent` (containment: "this task is part of this feature") and `blocks` (work-order dependency: "this must finish before that starts") are deliberately separate, orthogonal edge types -- a task is not expected to have a `blocks` edge to its own parent. So a grandchild that has a `parent` edge to its feature but no `blocks` edge to anything will render as an unindented top-level row in the Sprint section -- visually flat for the containment hierarchy, even though auto-sprint-3 already fixed which SECTION it lands in.

This is the piece of the user's "tree should look like a tree" concern not addressed by auto-sprint-3. Fixing it requires a design decision (not made unilaterally here): (a) switch renderBeadsHtml's nesting to use `parent` instead of/in addition to `blocks`, or (b) render both graphs (containment nesting, with blocks-order called out inline per node), or (c) something else the containment-vs-dependency semantics call for. Needs user/maintainer decision before implementing.

title: auto-sprint: planner/plan-reviewer self-query bd --parent directly -- diverges from orchestrator's now-recursive scope
description:
OBSERVED (per user's DRY/componentization concern -- "it should not happen that the viewer tree sees one view of the tree but planner doer etc. see something else"): after fixing auto-sprint-3 (bdListScoped rewritten to a full-project BFS so the orchestrator's dashboard/dispatch scope now correctly includes descendants at any depth), planner.md and plan-reviewer.md still instruct the LLM agent to independently run `bd list --parent <sprint-id> --ready --json` and `bd blocked --parent <sprint-id>` directly via their own Bash access (see planner.md lines ~107-121, plan-reviewer.md lines ~49-53).

`bd list --parent` is and remains single-level only (bd's own CLI, not the orchestrator's JS) -- there is no recursive flag. So when planner/plan-reviewer explore the tree themselves (as opposed to being handed a pre-computed scope as data), they still only see direct children, the exact same limitation bdListScoped had before the auto-sprint-3 fix. This means the orchestrator's dashboard/dispatch view and the planner/plan-reviewer's own self-directed exploration can now diverge on any sprint with 3+ levels of nesting (epic -> feature -> task), which apra-fleet-eft has.

doer.md and reviewer.md are NOT affected the same way -- they only use `bd show <id>` for a specific, already-named bead id (no tree exploration), not `bd list --parent`.

FIX DIRECTION (not yet implemented, needs a design decision): either (a) teach planner.md/plan-reviewer.md to recurse (e.g. instruct them to walk `--parent` transitively, or use `bd list --all` + manual filtering, mirroring the orchestrator's BFS), or (b) have the orchestrator hand the already-computed scope (bdListScoped's result) to these agents as data in their dispatch prompt, removing the need for them to independently query bd for structure at all -- (b) is more DRY and is the direction the user's message leans toward ("all parts of the code get the same view of the beads").

comments.author: Bot
comments.text: Independent fable-model review flagged this as more urgent than 'deferred, needs design call' implies: fixing the orchestrator's bdListScoped (auto-sprint-3) WIDENED this divergence rather than just leaving it unchanged -- both orchestrator and planner/plan-reviewer were single-level-wrong before (same wrong answer, no visible split); now only the LLM agents are, meaning the orchestrator can dispatch/track level-3+ tasks the planning agents structurally cannot see in their own bd --parent queries. Recommend resolving the design question (recurse in the agent prompts vs. hand agents the pre-computed scope as data) before the next real sprint relaunch, not purely at leisure.

title: auto-sprint: run4 (fleet-rev) doer streaks failing because local branch tracks wrong upstream, never fetched origin/auto-sprint/eft-service
description:
OBSERVED: during run4 (task b2lhcqlhh, --members fleet-rev), doer streaks depending on finalizeAbort() (eft.1, eft.1.2) reported the function does not exist, despite eft.1.1 being marked closed in beads and despite the function having been recovered/pushed to origin/auto-sprint/eft-service (commit bf203c3, earlier this session) after run1/run2's crashes.

DIAGNOSIS (read-only, confirmed via a diagnostic agent on fleet-rev, no changes made):
  - fleet-rev's local branch named "auto-sprint/eft-service" is checked out, HEAD at 8a1ef35 -- an ancestor of the real branch (predates all of eft.1.2/eft.2.1/eft.4.1/ire.1/the recovery commits).
  - `git branch -vv` shows this local branch's upstream is set to origin/feat/fleet-reorg, NOT origin/auto-sprint/eft-service.
  - `git log -1 origin/auto-sprint/eft-service` fails with "unknown revision" on fleet-rev -- its local git has NEVER fetched that remote ref at all, so it has zero knowledge bf203c3 or anything else pushed to that branch exists.
  - grep confirms finalizeAbort is genuinely absent in fleet-rev's working copy (consistent with being an old snapshot, not a code regression).

ROOT CAUSE: this is NOT the same-named-branch-reused-as-is case the auto-sprint docs describe (README.md: "if it already exists ... it is reused as-is") working as intended -- reuse-as-is only makes sense when the existing local branch is a real, fetched view of the same remote branch. Here, a local branch that happens to share the target branch NAME was created/left over from a DIFFERENT prior operation (its upstream points at feat/fleet-reorg), and the sprint's "Ensure Sprint Branch" phase accepted it without verifying it actually tracks/is up to date with origin's branch of the same name. This is a real gap in that phase's verification, not just bad luck.

IMPACT: any doer work in run4 on fleet-rev that depends on commits landed after 8a1ef35 (i.e. everything recovered this session: eft.1.2, eft.2.1 fix, eft.4.1 supervisor, ire.1 dolt-install, eft.2.2/2.3/5.1 WIP) will spuriously fail as "not found" / "does not exist", even though the actual apra-fleet-eft epic HAS this work done -- fleet-rev is just blind to it. Every such streak gets marked FAILED and consumes a develop round for no real reason.

FIX NEEDED (not yet applied -- flagging for a decision before continuing run4):
  1. Immediate/manual: on fleet-rev, `git fetch origin` then reconcile the local auto-sprint/eft-service branch to actually point at origin/auto-sprint/eft-service (e.g. `git branch -u origin/auto-sprint/eft-service auto-sprint/eft-service && git reset --hard origin/auto-sprint/eft-service` -- destructive to any fleet-rev-local-only commits on that branch name, so should be confirmed there are none first).
  2. Systemic: runner.js's "Ensure Sprint Branch" phase should verify the local branch's upstream matches the expected remote branch (and that it's up to date after a fetch) before treating "branch already exists" as sufficient to reuse as-is -- currently it appears to only check local branch-name presence.
notes: Root cause confirmed and fixed via auto-sprint-9 (commit 7c451f3). No manual git surgery needed on fleet-rev -- the next sprint launch's Ensure Sprint Branch phase will now fetch and correctly adopt origin/auto-sprint/eft-service there automatically.
close_reason: Root cause fixed via auto-sprint-9 (commit 7c451f3). Self-heals automatically on next sprint launch; no manual branch surgery needed.

title: Remote members need agents: onboarding doesn't provision .claude/agents/*.md role files to remote fleet members
description:
PROBLEM: Local fleet members sharing the operator's Windows user account (e.g. fleet-reorg, fleet-dev) transparently get all auto-sprint/pm role-agent definitions (planner.md, plan-reviewer.md, doer.md, reviewer.md, harvester.md, deployer.md, integ-test-runner.md, ci-watcher.md, plus _shared/ and schemas/ support dirs) via the shared global ~/.claude/agents/ directory. Remote members (e.g. fleet-rev, a separate macOS machine) have their own independent home directory and never receive these files as part of onboarding/registration.

OBSERVED FAILURE: launching an auto-sprint run with --members fleet-rev failed immediately at the Plan phase:
  execute_prompt: agent "planner" not found on "fleet-rev".
  Expected at:
    /Users/akhil/git/apra-fleet/.claude/agents/planner.md
    ~/.claude/agents/planner.md
This repeated for all 3 planning rounds until SprintPlanRejectedError correctly aborted the sprint (a clean failure, not a crash -- but still a hard blocker for any remote member).

WORKAROUND USED (manual, ad-hoc, not the real fix): packaged the local ~/.claude/agents/ directory into a tarball, pushed it to the remote member's work folder via the apra-fleet MCP send_files tool, extracted via execute_command. Got fleet-rev unblocked for one sprint but is not durable and won't survive a fresh remote registration.

RIGHT FIX DIRECTION (per operator, 2026-07-18): today's remote-member model treats the remote purely as a dumb SSH execution target (register-member.ts + SSH-based execute_command/execute_prompt), which is why nothing about the apra-fleet install itself -- including agent definitions -- ever lands there. The correct long-term direction is federation: register_member should, in a future revision, provision/install apra-fleet itself onto the remote member (not just push files ad hoc), so the member runs its own real apra-fleet install and stays in sync the same way local members do.

This is HALF-DONE WORK ALREADY ON THIS BRANCH (feat/fleet-reorg): see commit e959d7b "feat(fleet-reorg): hub-spoke migration + fleet-dashboard SaaS bootstrap/sync integration" -- introduced spoke-mode remote execution (src/hub-service/*, src/services/hub-client.ts, src/services/relay-executor.ts, src/services/token-issuer.ts, JWT-authenticated envelope relay, `apra-fleet join <member-jwt>`) as an alternative to SSH for remote members. Per that commit's own scoping note, src/hub-service/ was "retired to reference-only status: code and its tests are kept... but no further production feature work lands there" -- fleet-dashboard was decided to own workspace/project/member/secret persistence instead, and SSH was kept as the permanent first-class transport. Agent-definition provisioning (this issue) is exactly the kind of gap that a real spoke-mode install (vs. today's SSH-as-dumb-pipe model) would close naturally, since installing apra-fleet on the remote would bring its own bundled agent contracts the same way local installs do.

NOT decided yet: whether reviving/finishing spoke-mode specifically for this purpose is in scope, or whether a lighter provisioning step gets bolted onto the current SSH-based onboarding sequence in the meantime. Needs a design decision before implementation -- flagging the connection to e959d7b/src/hub-service so whoever picks this up doesn't duplicate that groundwork.

IMPACT: any remote fleet member is currently unusable as an auto-sprint doer/planner/reviewer/etc. until the manual workaround is applied by hand. This will hit every new remote member registration going forward.
close_reason: Fixed by PR #336 (merged to main as 6e62608): per-OS+provider agent-file hash probe + provisioning on register/update member

comments.author: Bot
comments.text: Fix implemented and under review: PR #336 (fix/remote-agent-file-provisioning, 2 commits: 34327d58 + 8a12a754). Hash-based OS x provider provisioning at register/update time; suite 1693/0; adversarial review verdict SHIP with findings addressed.

title: auto-sprint: doer test-cleanup taskkill commands are a plausible (not yet proven) mechanism for killing the orchestrator's node.exe
description:
CONTEXT: following up on auto-sprint-4 (P0: two consecutive eft sprint runs died silently, no crash signature, no uncaught exception/unhandled rejection in the log -- ruled out via direct read of cli.mjs's error handling, which logs 'Sprint failed:' or the outer main().catch on every legitimate failure path, neither of which ever fired). User suggested checking whether a test in this repo kills node processes broadly enough to catch the orchestrator itself.

FINDING: no repo SOURCE/test file contains a hardcoded broad taskkill/pkill/Stop-Process (confirmed via repo-wide grep, apra-fleet-client's two hits are targeted: a liveness-check `process.kill(pid, 0)` and a specific child-handle `.kill()`). But AGENT-EXECUTED Bash commands (not baked into the repo, run ad-hoc by doers mid-turn) do this routinely:
  - A subagent on 2026-07-12 ran a genuinely broad `taskkill //F //IM node.exe` (kills EVERY node.exe on the machine by image name) -- 6 days before either crash, so not a direct hit, but proves this exact dangerous pattern has been used in this environment before.
  - Doer session a5099188-08bf-40ed-9e19-2a4f55fb39a3 ([yysru], working eft.2.2/eft.2.3 during run2) ran `taskkill //F //PID 50600 //T; taskkill //F //PID 26192 //T` at 2026-07-18T06:53:11Z (~02:53:11 local) -- PID-targeted (not broad-by-name), but `//T` kills the entire process tree rooted at each PID, and this ran only ~10 minutes before run2's orchestrator log went silent (03:03:08 local). The doer's own narration shows it was cleaning up a background test-server process it had itself started (eft.2.2/2.3 test run), matching the exact 'stale port-holding node.exe, manually taskkill'd' pattern already documented in apra-fleet-eft.13.

NOT PROVEN: I did not capture the orchestrator's own node.exe PID at launch time for either run1 (b0wm0tejb) or run2 (baspge4p1), so I cannot directly confirm either taskkill's target PID(s) matched the orchestrator's actual PID. Windows PID reuse (a PID captured via tasklist/netstat by the doer, then killed after the OS had already recycled that same PID number for an unrelated process -- e.g. the orchestrator, launched around the same time) is a plausible race that would explain an accidental hit without any broad by-name kill being involved at all.

RECOMMENDATION FOR NEXT RUN (not yet done, needs a decision on whether/when to relaunch):
  1. Immediately after launching, capture and log the orchestrator's own node.exe PID (e.g. `Get-CimInstance Win32_Process -Filter "name='node.exe'" | ? CommandLine -match 'cli.mjs'`).
  2. Poll `Get-CimInstance Win32_Process -Filter "name='node.exe'"` (PID, CreationDate, CommandLine, ParentProcessId) every 2-3 minutes for the whole run, so any future death can be cross-referenced PID-for-PID against every taskkill any doer ran in that window (grep each active session transcript for `taskkill`/`Stop-Process` the same way this investigation did, but now with a ground-truth orchestrator PID to compare against instead of only timestamp proximity).
  3. Separately, regardless of whether this specific mechanism is confirmed: recommend the fix direction already on file in apra-fleet-eft.13 (ephemeral test ports, best-effort scoped process-tree teardown) plus a stronger doer-level guardrail against `taskkill //IM node.exe` / any unscoped-by-name process kill, given it has already been used at least once in this environment's history.

title: auto-sprint: branch ref silently rewound during run2, discarding 3 unpushed commits (recoverable via reflog, not pushed/lost)
description:
OBSERVED: Between run1's death and run2's launch, three commits that were HEAD of auto-sprint/eft-service (fffc299, 2062e5a, and my own manual recovery commit 72e504e -- see auto-sprint-2) disappeared from the branch entirely. `git log --all --oneline` finds none of them; the branch now shows a DIFFERENT history: c912f16 -> 1497bbe (Merge remote-tracking branch 'origin/auto-sprint/eft-service' into auto-sprint/eft-service) -> {898f406, 6cc1867/67157db as parallel parents}.

This was independently surfaced by run2's own eft.1.3 doer, who correctly refused to write a test for finalizeAbort: "apra-fleet-eft.1.2 ... shows as closed in beads, but a full-repo grep found no finalizeAbort symbol, no [ABORTED] PR-title string, and no abort-handling logic anywhere ... recommend the orchestrator verify whether eft.1.1/eft.1.2 commits were lost/reverted or landed on a different branch before re-dispatching eft.1.3." -- i.e. beads state (eft.1.2 = closed) and actual branch content (no finalizeAbort code) went out of sync.

RECOVERABLE: all three commits still exist as valid, uncorrupted git objects and appear in `git reflog --all` (auto-sprint/eft-service@{4}, @{5}, @{6} in this checkout's reflog) -- not garbage collected, not permanently lost. `git cat-file -t <hash>` confirms all three resolve to type "commit".

ANALYSIS: I did not run any git reset/checkout/rebase between committing 72e504e and launching run2 (`node bin/cli.mjs ...`) -- the only git-affecting action in between was the sprint launch itself. The merge commit 1497bbe's message ("Merge remote-tracking branch 'origin/...' into ...") indicates something inside the sprint's own startup (most likely the '--- Phase: Ensure Sprint Branch ---' step in runner.js, or a doer mid-turn) fetched origin and merged/reset in a way that used origin's OLDER tip (still at 67157db/6cc1867, since fffc299/2062e5a/72e504e were never pushed) as a parent, effectively stranding the 3 local-only commits off any ref. This needs runner.js's branch-sync logic reviewed directly (not yet done) to confirm exactly which git command performed the rewind -- flagging the symptom now rather than guessing the exact mechanism.

RISK: the dangling commits are NOT protected from `git gc` indefinitely -- default gc.pruneExpire is 2 weeks for reflog-referenced objects, shorter if gc is invoked with --aggressive/--prune=now. Recommend recovering (cherry-pick or merge fffc299 and 72e504e onto the current branch tip, resolving conflicts with the newer eft.4.1/ire.1 work as needed) before any gc runs, or before assuming this branch's history is complete.

NOT YET DONE: I have not attempted the recovery merge myself -- surfacing this for a human decision first, since the branch has since diverged further (ire.1, eft.4.1 both landed after the rewind) and a blind merge could conflict.
notes: Root cause confirmed via auto-sprint-9 (commit 7c451f3, now fixed): the Ensure Sprint Branch phase's unconditional 'checkout -B <branch> origin/<baseBranch>' was silently discarding unpushed local commits on every launch. Recovered commits (72e504e/fffc299/2062e5a) were already cherry-picked and pushed earlier this session; future launches will no longer cause this class of loss.
close_reason: Root cause fixed via auto-sprint-9 (commit 7c451f3). Self-heals automatically on next sprint launch; no manual branch surgery needed.

title: auto-sprint: dashboard/dispatch scope (bdListScoped) only sees direct children -- level-3+ eft.X.Y tasks invisible to sprintTasks and readyLeafBeads()
description:
OBSERVED: User noticed a whole lot of level-3 eft items (e.g. apra-fleet-eft.1.2, eft.6.7) do not show in the sprint dashboard tree, even though the plan-reviewer's own report and the Streak Assignment log line both reference them by id.

ROOT CAUSE (confirmed empirically, not guessed):
`bd list --parent <id>` is single-level only -- it returns DIRECT children of <id>, never grandchildren. Confirmed:
  - `bd list --parent apra-fleet-eft --json` -> exactly 13 ids: eft.1..eft.13 (the level-2 features/bugs). Zero level-3 ids.
  - `bd list --parent apra-fleet-eft.1 --json` -> eft.1.2, eft.1.3 (the level-3 leaf tasks) -- proving the leaf tasks exist and are correctly parented, just not reachable from a --parent query rooted at the epic.
  - `bd list --help` confirms: no recursive/depth flag exists at all (`--parent string  Filter by parent issue ID (shows children of specified issue)`).

IMPACT SITE: packages/apra-fleet-se/auto-sprint/runner.js
  - `bdListScoped()` (lines 1168-1185) is the ONLY scoping primitive in the file and always issues `bd list --parent <targetIssues[i]> ...` where targetIssues = the --issue flag value(s) (here, just the epic apra-fleet-eft). Structurally, its result set can never contain a level-3 grandchild of that epic.
  - `updateDashboard()` (line 1417+): `sprintTasks = await bdListScoped('--json')` feeds the dashboard tree directly (via viewer-extensions.mjs renderBeadsHtml). Its own comment claims sprintTasks is 'everything under this sprint's target scope' -- that claim is false whenever the sprint target has 3+ levels of nesting (epic -> feature -> leaf task), which apra-fleet-eft does.
  - `readyLeafBeads()` (lines 1200-1207), which feeds the REAL Develop-loop dispatch (readyBeads at line 1836, currentReady at line 1879 -- this is not just a display bug): computes `parentIds = allInScope.filter(b => b.parent).map(b => b.parent)` and filters ready items whose id is itself a parent, to avoid dispatching a grouping node instead of a leaf. But since allInScope (via bdListScoped) only ever contains the 13 level-2 items, parentIds only ever contains "apra-fleet-eft" (their shared parent, the epic, which is out of scope and never in `ready` anyway) -- so this filter can never actually exclude a level-2 feature/bug bead from readyBeads even though those beads DO have real children (level-3 leaves) and per the file's own xbu.C5 invariant should never be dispatched directly alongside their subtasks.

OPEN QUESTION (not yet resolved, needs runner.js author or further tracing): the observed Streak Assignment log line `{"streaks":[["apra-fleet-eft.1.2","apra-fleet-eft.1"],...]}` DOES reference the level-3 leaf apra-fleet-eft.1.2 -- so something in the actual Develop-phase dispatch path evidently reaches level-3 tasks correctly (likely the Streak Assignment / doer LLM independently runs its own `bd list --parent eft.1` or `bd show` inside its dispatched turn, since those agents have their own Bash/bd access, separate from the orchestrator's programmatic bdListScoped calls). If so, the impact may be limited to the DASHBOARD display (real functional bug, but cosmetic) rather than actual dispatch correctness -- but this needs to be verified by re-reading the Streak Assignment prompt-construction code and the actual doer's session transcript, not assumed either way.

RELATED: this is the same code area (runner.js dispatch/scoping) implicated in the crash under investigation (auto-sprint-2) and in apra-fleet-eft.13 (a PRIOR, separately-filed bug: EADDRINUSE fixed-port cascade + orphaned-process-holds-port-on-retry, root-caused from a DIFFERENT earlier failed eft sprint attempt, describing an near-identical 'sprint gives up entirely once streak dispatch starts failing' pattern). Worth having whoever fixes eft.13 also check whether the bdListScoped single-level limitation contributed to that earlier failure's scope-tracking confusion.

FIX DIRECTION: bdListScoped (or a new recursive variant) should transitively expand scope -- e.g. BFS/DFS: start from targetIssues, `bd list --parent <id>` each newly-discovered id, union all results, until no new ids are found -- rather than a single one-level query. readyLeafBeads()'s parentIds computation would then correctly see level-3 parents too.
close_reason: Fixed: replaced single-level bd list --parent scoping with a full-project listing + in-memory parent/child BFS in bdListScoped() (packages/apra-fleet-se/auto-sprint/runner.js). sprintTasks/readyLeafBeads() now correctly include descendants at any depth. Also fixed a concurrency bug this rewrite introduced (duplicate concurrent bd list --all calls colliding in the bd-replay test shim's FIFO matching) by request-coalescing concurrent callers onto one in-flight fetch. Full suite (346 tests) passes deterministically at default and serial concurrency. Committed f0188fe on auto-sprint/eft-service, pushed. This also root-causes and fixes the separately-reported 'eft grandchildren show as Backlog' dashboard symptom (updateDashboard()'s BACKLOG_STATUSES exclusion relied on the same incomplete sprintTasks).

title: auto-sprint: eft sprint process died silently mid-Develop-phase, no error logged, leaving uncommitted work
description:
OBSERVED: The auto-sprint run for apra-fleet-eft (task b0wm0tejb, launched via bin/cli.mjs --issue apra-fleet-eft --members fleet-reorg --branch auto-sprint/eft-service --base feat/fleet-reorg --viewer-port 18300) stopped partway through Develop Cycle 1 Round 1. Harness reported status "killed" with no user action taken to stop it.

TIMELINE:
1. Sprint Setup + Plan C1 R1 completed normally: plan-reviewer returned verdict APPROVED with full task-tier assignments logged.
2. Develop C1 R1 began: streak assignment computed (14 streaks across eft.1-eft.13 + apra-fleet-ire), first streak [apra-fleet-eft.1.2, apra-fleet-eft.1] dispatched to fleet-reorg.
3. Log ends immediately after that dispatch line -- no error, exception, or shutdown message of any kind. Last log write: 2026-07-17 23:40:08.
4. Two commits DID land on the branch before death (fffc299 "fix(apra-fleet-eft.2.1): make SIGINT test mock honor abort signal", 2062e5a "feat(auto-sprint-eft.1.2): route typed sprint-abort errors through finalizeAbort + terminal history record") -- so real work happened past what the log shows, meaning the log itself stopped updating before the process actually died (or output buffering hid later lines).
5. At kill time: dashboard port 18300 no longer LISTENING, no fleet-se node process alive at OS level, and the working tree was left with uncommitted changes:
   - modified: packages/apra-fleet-se/auto-sprint/docs/README.md
   - untracked: .tmp-s8-log.txt, .tmp/, test/dispatch-safety-guard.test.mjs

ANALYSIS:
1. No crash signature (no stack trace, no "Error:", no exit-code line) appears anywhere in the captured log -- this looks like the process was terminated externally (SIGTERM/SIGKILL from outside the script), not a script-level fault. I launched it via Bash run_in_background specifically to avoid the "tool-call process group killed on return" failure mode the docs/README.md itself warns about -- worth checking whether that failure mode occurred anyway (e.g. session/job boundary reaping background processes), which would mean the harness-tracked backgrounding does NOT actually protect against it on this platform.
2. The README.md diff left uncommitted is notable: a doer streak (either eft.1.2 or eft.2.1, neither of which is "update the example launch command in this doc") edited the top-of-file example to match this exact session's invocation (issue apra-fleet-eft, branch auto-sprint/eft-service, base feat/fleet-reorg, log path changed to ./sprint-logs/auto-sprint.log). This is scope creep outside the dispatched task's stated acceptance criteria and, combined with auto-sprint-1's finding that this same file steers agents away from the sanctioned Workflow tool, is worth a human looking at directly -- an agent silently keeping this file's example "fresh" to match whatever was last run is a way for the file's instructions to stay perpetually plausible/current-looking regardless of whether they're still correct.
3. Uncommitted work is NOT lost (git status confirms working tree still has it) but is at risk if anything runs git clean/reset before it's triaged. Recommend a human (or a fresh, non-orphaned sprint continuation) reviews and either commits or discards packages/apra-fleet-se/auto-sprint/docs/README.md and test/dispatch-safety-guard.test.mjs before relaunching.
4. NEXT STEP recommended: relaunch on the same branch (auto-sprint/eft-service, already reused-as-is per README's own documented semantics) but capture whether the process dies at the same point again -- reproducibility would point to a resource/env issue (e.g. port 18300 conflict, OOM) rather than a one-off external kill.

title: Fixed-port test servers in apra-fleet-workflow cause EADDRINUSE cascade -> sprint-wide MCP transport timeout
description:
Root-caused via the fleet-reorg member's actual Claude Code session transcripts (~/.claude/projects/C--akhil-git-apra-fleet-reorg/) after the apra-fleet-eft auto-sprint (branch auto-sprint/eft-service) FAILED: every doer streak from eft.2 onward hit "Transport closed" / "tools/call timed out after 900000ms", retried once, failed again, until the sprint gave up entirely.

TIMELINE:
- Session ee09231b-7afd-48a2-a296-2c49cb88774f: eft.1.1 doer completes cleanly at 2026-07-17T07:39 UTC, commits 7b756ce.
- Session 03816911-3cf2-47cc-99a6-7468a061d9ab (the very next dispatch, eft.2.1 doer): spends the rest of its turn diagnosing an EADDRINUSE-style port conflict on port 18099/18100 -- leftover node.exe processes (observed PIDs 49144, 49260, 29388) from a PRIOR abandoned attempt were still holding those ports. The doer polled with `sleep 20` / `timeout 90 bash -c 'while kill -0 <pid> ...'`, then manually taskkill'd the stale PIDs before it could proceed. This legitimately exceeded the fleet's 900s MCP tools/call timeout even though the CLI session was still alive and working.

ROOT CAUSE (two-fold):
1. packages/apra-fleet-workflow's test suite hardcodes FIXED ports for createDashboardViewer() test servers instead of ephemeral (port: 0):
   - test/apra-fleet-workflow-debounced-writer.test.mjs: 18099, 18100, 18101 (eft.2.1's own feature test)
   - test/apra-fleet-workflow-viewer-lifecycle.test.mjs: 18091-18098
   - test/test-runner.test.mjs: 18080
   - test/manual/e2e-runner.mjs: 18081
   Any two overlapping/retried runs of the same test file collide on the same port.
2. When the fleet's MCP tools/call request times out waiting on a doer turn, runner.js's dispatch-exception retry (apra-fleet-02s.1) spawns a FRESH claude CLI invocation for the same work -- but the PREVIOUS CLI invocation's own backgrounded child processes (e.g. `(node --test ... > /tmp/out.txt 2>&1 &)`, spawned by the doer itself mid-turn) are never killed, since the timeout only tears down the fleet-side transport, not the underlying OS process tree. Those orphans then hold the SAME fixed port for the retry too, so the retry stalls out identically and times out again -- spawning yet more orphans. This is a self-reinforcing cascade, not a transient blip, which is why the sprint never recovered once it started at eft.2 and every subsequent streak failed the same way.

FIX DIRECTION:
- Change every hardcoded createDashboardViewer test port above to `port: 0` (OS-assigned ephemeral) and read the actual bound port back via `server.address().port`, mirroring the NOTE already present at test-runner.test.mjs:196-199 (which deliberately chose a fixed port there for a different, narrower reason -- worth re-checking whether that reasoning still holds once the others are fixed).
- Separately/defense-in-depth: runner.js's dispatch-exception retry path (or execute-prompt.ts's dispatch layer) should make a best-effort attempt to terminate the abandoned CLI invocation's process tree on a transport timeout, not just abandon it -- so a slow-but-alive turn cannot leave orphaned child processes behind for the retry to collide with.
- Add regression coverage: two back-to-back/overlapping runs of the same fixed-port test file (simulating a retry-after-timeout scenario) must not EADDRINUSE.
acceptance_criteria: Every hardcoded createDashboardViewer test-server port in packages/apra-fleet-workflow tests is changed to port:0 (OS-assigned ephemeral) with the actual bound port read back via server.address().port -- covering debounced-writer.test.mjs (18099/18100/18101), viewer-lifecycle.test.mjs (18091-18098), test-runner.test.mjs (18080) and manual/e2e-runner.mjs (18081); the deliberate fixed-port NOTE at test-runner.test.mjs:196-199 is re-evaluated and either kept with a written rationale or converted to ephemeral. The full apra-fleet-workflow suite passes even when the same test file is run twice concurrently/retried, with no EADDRINUSE. Defense-in-depth: the dispatch-exception retry path (runner.js or execute-prompt.ts) makes a best-effort terminate of the previous CLI invocation's OS process tree on a transport timeout so orphaned doer children no longer hold the same port across a retry. npm test passes; source is ASCII-only.
close_reason: integ-test-runner (2026-07-21): no longer reproduces. packages/apra-fleet-workflow/test/apra-fleet-workflow-ephemeral-ports.test.mjs (eft.13.4, 3/3 pass via 'node --test'): back-to-back, overlapping, and 5x-repeated runs of a formerly-fixed-port test file each bind a distinct ephemeral port with no EADDRINUSE even while all old fixed ports 18080-18101 are held by an orphan.

title: Dashboard viewer misclassifies grandchild+ sprint tasks as Backlog (bd list --parent is single-level, unlike --ready)
description:
Found live during the running apra-fleet-eft auto-sprint (dashboard http://localhost:18310, branch auto-sprint/eft-service): every leaf-level task bead (e.g. apra-fleet-eft.1.1, two levels below the epic under feature apra-fleet-eft.1) appears in the dashboard's "Backlog" section instead of nested under "Sprint", even while actively being dispatched, closed, and committed by the running sprint.

ROOT CAUSE (confirmed via direct bd calls against this repo's real DB):
- `bd list --parent apra-fleet-eft --json` (plain, no --ready) returns ONLY the 12 direct-child features (eft.1..eft.12) -- it does NOT recurse into their own children (the 46 actual task-level leaves).
- `bd list --parent apra-fleet-eft --ready --json` DOES recurse through the full descendant tree (returns leaf tasks like eft.1.2, eft.4.1, eft.3.1, etc. alongside the features) -- so bd's own --parent filter behaves inconsistently depending on whether --ready is also passed.
- packages/apra-fleet-se/auto-sprint/runner.js's bdListScoped() (~line 1290) and updateDashboard() (~line 1539) call the PLAIN (non---ready) form to build sprintTasks for the dashboard's beads panel. Since leaf tasks are absent from sprintTasks, updateDashboard()'s backlogTasks computation (~line 1575-1580, "everything open/deferred project-wide MINUS sprintIds") then incorrectly captures them as backlog, even though they belong to the sprint and have no relation to any other epic.

SCOPE OF IMPACT: this is a DASHBOARD/VIEWER bug only. Real dispatch/streak-assignment logic already uses --ready-flavored queries elsewhere in runner.js, which DO recurse correctly -- confirmed by the running eft sprint successfully dispatching and closing apra-fleet-eft.1.1 (a grandchild task) in this exact run. No dispatch-scope or sprint-completion logic is affected, only the dashboard's visual Sprint/Backlog split.

FIX DIRECTION: bdListScoped()'s plain (non-ready) call needs to become recursive -- either by unioning bd list --parent <id> --json across every feature-level child returned by the top-level query (walk one extra level), or by finding/using a genuinely recursive bd query if one exists (bd list --help currently shows --parent as single-level-only with no descendant/recursive flag). Whichever fix path is chosen, add regression coverage asserting a 3-level epic->feature->task hierarchy renders its leaf tasks nested under Sprint, not Backlog.
acceptance_criteria: bdListScoped()'s plain (non---ready) call in packages/apra-fleet-se/auto-sprint/runner.js becomes recursive (union bd list --parent across every feature-level child, or an equivalent genuinely-recursive query) so that leaf task beads two or more levels below the epic appear nested under Sprint in the dashboard, not under Backlog. updateDashboard()'s backlogTasks computation no longer captures in-sprint leaf tasks. A regression test asserts that a 3-level epic->feature->task hierarchy renders its leaf tasks under Sprint (not Backlog). Dispatch-scope and sprint-completion logic remain unchanged. npm test passes; source is ASCII-only.
close_reason: Root-cause fix (bdListScoped recursive BFS) was already merged as commit 7fcebd1a (auto-sprint-3); added the missing regression test asserting a 3-level epic->feature->task hierarchy renders its leaf task under Sprint, not Backlog, in updateDashboard()'s published beads state.

title: [test] reservation ledger: both-axis overlap rejection, live subtree recompute, restart reconcile, force-release
description:
Files: packages/apra-fleet-se/test/ (new supervisor-reservation test).

Cover: (a) member overlap (including an orchestrator-only overlap) -> 409 naming the sprint and members; (b) issue-scope overlap -> 409 naming the sprint and bead ids; (c) a child bead created after launch under a claimed root is still detected (proves live recomputation, not a snapshot); (d) both axes claim/release atomically on every terminal path -- success, fail, abort, crash-reconciled; (e) ledger survives supervisor restart and PID reconciliation releases dead-child reservations; (f) force-release works; (g) intra-sprint memberLocks behavior unchanged.
acceptance_criteria: All seven cases pass; case (c) fails if the implementation is switched to a launch-time snapshot; case (d) is parameterized over all four terminal paths; the suite asserts rejections name both the conflicting sprint id and the specific overlapping members/bead ids.
close_reason: Closed

title: [impl] Restart reconciliation (PID probe -> release both axes, mark aborted-by-restart) and force-release endpoint
description:
Files: src/supervisor/ledger + supervisor startup path (pairs with eft.4.5) + an API route.

On supervisor restart, PID-probe each ledger entry: dead children release BOTH axes in one atomic write and their sprint is marked aborted-by-restart in history; live children keep their reservations (re-adopted by eft.4.5). Add POST /api/reservations/:sprintId/force-release for wedged states, which releases both axes and records who/why.
acceptance_criteria: After a restart with one live and one dead recorded child, the dead child's members AND issueRoots are both released in a single atomic write and its sprint shows aborted-by-restart; the live child keeps both axes reserved; force-release frees both axes of a wedged entry and records an audit reason; force-releasing an unknown sprint id 404s.
close_reason: Closed

title: [impl] Issue-scope overlap via live-expanded subtree recomputation (reuse bdListScoped)
description:
Files: src/supervisor/ledger scope module; reuse the bdListScoped pattern (runner.js ~1168, 'bd list --parent <root> --json') -- do NOT reinvent subtree expansion.

Identity key is the root issue id(s) the sprint launched with. At every launch attempt, RECOMPUTE the live-expanded subtree for every active sprint's roots and intersect with the incoming request's live-expanded scope -- never a frozen launch-time snapshot, because planners/reviewers grow the subtree mid-run. Any nonzero intersection rejects the ENTIRE launch, naming the conflicting sprint and the overlapping bead ids. No partial-launch carve-out (that needs an exclude-set threaded through the runner core loop; explicitly deferred). Note bd list --parent takes exactly ONE id per call: query each root separately and merge. Known hole to surface, not hide: blocks-edges crossing two disjoint claimed scopes are not detected.
acceptance_criteria: Overlap check re-expands subtrees at launch-attempt time: a bead created AFTER launch under an already-claimed root is still detected as overlapping (explicit test); any nonzero intersection rejects the whole launch with the conflicting sprint id and overlapping bead ids listed; multiple roots are expanded via one bd list --parent call each and merged (never a comma-joined --parent); no partial/carve-out launch path is introduced.
close_reason: Closed

title: [impl] Member-axis overlap check on POST /api/sprints: all-or-nothing union claim with 409 conflict
description:
Files: src/supervisor/ledger + the POST /api/sprints route (eft.4.4).

At launch, atomically reserve the FULL member set = union of --members and every roleMap value including the orchestrator. All-or-nothing: any overlap with an active sprint rejects the whole launch with a 409-style response naming the conflicting sprint id and the specific overlapping member names. Confirmed member economics: whole-sprint member-per-role reservation even while a role is idle; no role-level hand-off release in v1.
acceptance_criteria: Launching with any member already reserved by an active sprint returns 409 naming the conflicting sprint id and the overlapping member names; the union includes roleMap values and the orchestrator (test asserts an orchestrator-only overlap is caught); a rejected launch leaves the ledger byte-identical (no partial claim); a non-overlapping launch claims every member in the union.
close_reason: Verified: defaultMemberOverlapGuard (api.mjs) already implements the all-or-nothing 409 union check; all 4 acceptance criteria (direct overlap, orchestrator-only overlap, byte-identical rejection, non-overlapping claims union) have passing tests in supervisor-api.test.mjs (409 overlap check describe block). No code changes needed.

title: [impl] Combined reservation ledger module: lockstep member+issueRoots claim/release, atomic disk persistence
description:
Files: new src/supervisor/ledger module (plugs into the eft.4.1 supervisor seams).

ONE combined ledger per plan Part 2.2: reservations: { [sprintId]: { members, issueRoots, childPid, reservedAt } }. Both axes claim in exact lockstep -- the same launch event claims members AND issueRoots, the same terminal event releases both, in ONE atomic write, with ONE restart-reconciliation path. Persist to disk under the service data dir with an atomic write (temp file + rename). Export claim(), release(), get(), list() and the persisted-shape schema for the other tasks to build on. Intra-sprint memberLocks in runner.js is orthogonal and must stay untouched.
acceptance_criteria: claim() writes both axes or neither (asserted by an injected mid-write failure); release() clears both; the on-disk file is updated atomically (no torn read observable by a concurrent reader); the ledger reloads exactly from disk across process restart; runner.js memberLocks is unmodified.
close_reason: Closed

title: [test] supervisor lifecycle: detach survival, four statuses, restart re-adoption
description:
Files: packages/apra-fleet-se/test/ (new supervisor-lifecycle test; use the background-supervised real integration test runner added in 42ea354 where a real process is needed).

Cover end-to-end: (a) supervisor stays up across a sprint completing and only exits on /api/shutdown; (b) detached child survives supervisor SIGKILL; (c) each of the four statuses is exercised -- healthy child, hung child (stop answering HTTP but stay alive), killed child (no terminal state), completed child (terminal state in old_sprints/); (d) restart re-adopts the live child by PID.
acceptance_criteria: All four scenarios pass, each asserting the exact status string; the hung-child case asserts running-unresponsive (not crashed); real processes are used for detach/kill/re-adopt cases rather than mocks; the suite cleans up every spawned PID even on failure.
close_reason: Closed

title: [impl] Supervisor restart: re-adopt live children by PID from the persisted ledger
description:
Files: new src/supervisor/ startup/reconciliation path; reads the persisted ledger from eft.5.

On startup the supervisor loads the persisted ledger and PID-probes every recorded sprint: live PIDs are re-adopted (tracked, watchdog-monitored, HTTP-reachable via their recorded viewer port), dead PIDs are handed to the reconciliation path (eft.5.4). Confirmed scope: detach-and-PID-readopt is the target; sprints need NOT survive machine restart; no journal/replay productization.
acceptance_criteria: After SIGKILL of the supervisor and a restart, still-live children appear in GET /api/sprints with correct status and working state proxying (integration test with a real detached child); dead recorded children are not reported as running; re-adoption uses only the persisted ledger plus PID probing (no IPC, no journal replay).
close_reason: Closed

title: [impl] Supervisor HTTP endpoints: members, backlog, sprints CRUD, stop proxy
description:
Files: new src/supervisor/api routes module; reuse exported helpers validateIssueId/validateBranchName (runner.js ~241/256) and resolveRoleMap.

Implement: GET /api/members (list_members plus reservation overlay), GET /api/backlog, POST /api/sprints (validated launch; forwards the per-request goal -- already a per-invocation cli.mjs flag -- into child argv), GET /api/sprints and GET /api/sprints/:id (live state proxied from the child /state, falling back to history when not live), POST /api/sprints/:id/stop (proxies the child's /stop). Request validation MUST reuse the exported runner.js helpers rather than reimplementing id/branch validation.
acceptance_criteria: All six endpoints implemented and covered by request-validation tests; invalid issue ids/branch names are rejected with a 400 naming the field, using the imported validateIssueId/validateBranchName (no duplicated regexes); POST /api/sprints forwards the per-request goal into child argv (asserted on the spawn args); GET /api/sprints/:id returns live child state when running and the historical record when finished; POST /api/sprints/:id/stop reaches the child's /stop endpoint.
close_reason: Closed

title: [impl] PID-liveness watchdog and four-status classifier (healthy/unresponsive/crashed/finished)
description:
Files: new src/supervisor/watchdog module.

Per plan Part 2.1: same-instance exit events are authoritative, but supervisor RESTART is the severance point, so a watchdog on a short interval PID-probes every ledger-listed sprint and combines PID liveness with child HTTP reachability into EXACTLY four statuses: running-healthy (PID alive + HTTP answering), running-unresponsive (PID alive, HTTP silent -> operator attention, NOT auto-declared crashed), crashed (PID gone, no terminal state persisted), finished (PID gone, terminal state in history i.e. old_sprints/). Do not auto-kill or auto-restart anything.
acceptance_criteria: The classifier returns exactly one of the four documented statuses for each ledger-listed sprint; a hung child (PID alive, HTTP not answering) classifies as running-unresponsive and is never auto-declared crashed or killed; PID-gone with a terminal state in old_sprints/ classifies finished, without one classifies crashed; PID reuse is guarded (probe validates the PID is plausibly our child, not any process); watchdog interval is configurable.
close_reason: Closed

title: [impl] Detached child-per-sprint spawner with per-sprint --viewer-port allocation
description:
Files: new src/supervisor/spawner module; reads packages/apra-fleet-se/bin/cli.mjs argv contract (--viewer-port etc., see cli.mjs ~line 111/399).

Spawn each sprint as bin/cli.mjs via spawn({ detached: true, stdio: 'ignore' }) with an allocated free --viewer-port unique per live sprint, and child.unref(). Record the child PID for the ledger. Children must be true independently-surviving orphans: killing the supervisor must not kill them, and a crashing sprint must not take down siblings or the service.
acceptance_criteria: Each launched sprint runs as a detached bin/cli.mjs child with a unique free viewer port; killing the supervisor (SIGKILL) leaves children running (test asserts PID still alive); killing one child leaves siblings and the supervisor unaffected; the child PID is returned to the caller for ledger recording; port allocation never hands the same port to two live sprints.
close_reason: Implemented detached child-per-sprint spawner (src/supervisor/spawner.mjs) with OS-verified unique --viewer-port allocation, pid return, and true-orphan (detached+unref, no kill-on-teardown) semantics; 18 new tests incl. 2 real-subprocess SIGKILL-survival tests, full suite 365/365 pass.

title: [impl] Supervisor skeleton: 'fleet-se serve' always-on process, HTTP server bootstrap, POST /api/shutdown
description:
Files: packages/apra-fleet-se/bin/ (new serve entry) plus a new src/supervisor/ module (server + module layout).

Stand up the always-on supervisor per plan Part 2.1 process model B: a long-lived process exposing an HTTP API on a fixed service port, structured so the ledger (eft.5), spawner, watchdog and dashboard (eft.6) plug in as modules. Runs indefinitely; exits ONLY on POST /api/shutdown or an explicit signal -- never because a sprint finished, and never because a child crashed. fork()/IPC is explicitly rejected; do not introduce a parent-child IPC channel. Define the module boundaries other tasks in this epic depend on.
acceptance_criteria: 'fleet-se serve' starts and stays up with zero sprints and after a sprint completes; POST /api/shutdown terminates it cleanly; an unhandled error inside one request never exits the process; module seams for ledger/spawner/watchdog/dashboard are exported and documented in the module header; no fork()/IPC channel is created.
close_reason: Closed

title: [test] continuous persistence survives hard kill and running/old_sprints membership is authoritative
description:
Files: packages/apra-fleet-se/test/ or packages/apra-fleet-workflow/test/ (follow apra-fleet-workflow-viewer-lifecycle.test.mjs conventions).

Cover: (a) SIGKILL a sprint mid-run -> the running/<sprintId>.json on disk reflects all progress up to at most one debounce window before the kill; (b) a completed sprint has no file in running/ and exactly one in old_sprints/; (c) the enriched fields are present; (d) sprint-logs/ output is unchanged vs the pre-change golden.
acceptance_criteria: All four cases pass under npm test; the hard-kill case uses a real SIGKILL (not a mocked exit) and asserts the bound of at most one debounce window of lost progress; the test fails if the writer is reverted to end-only persistence.
close_reason: Closed

title: [impl] running/ -> old_sprints/ layout under the service data dir, keyed by sprint id
description:
Files: packages/apra-fleet-workflow/src/viewer/index.mjs plus a small path-resolution helper module.

Write the live sprint file to <serviceDataDir>/running/<sprintId>.json, overwritten in place (one file per sprint, keyed by sprint id -- NOT the HHMMSS key, which collides across days). On terminal completion, move the file to <serviceDataDir>/old_sprints/<sprintId>.json so live-vs-finished is a directory-membership check with no stale 'isRunning' field. The service data dir must resolve outside the repo checkout (do not write old_sprints/ into the working tree).
acceptance_criteria: Live sprint state appears at <dataDir>/running/<sprintId>.json and is overwritten in place; on completion the file is moved (not copied) to <dataDir>/old_sprints/<sprintId>.json; no state file is written into the repo checkout; two sprints started in the same second on different days never collide; sprint-logs/ still lands in the checkout as before.
close_reason: Already implemented and tested in commit 08c8fe1a (sprint-state-paths.mjs resolves running/<sprintId>.json and old_sprints/<sprintId>.json under getFleetDataDir(), moveStateToOldSprints() renames on terminal completion). Verified via apra-fleet-workflow-sprint-state.test.mjs (all 3 tests pass) plus full workflow package suite (143/143 pass).

title: [impl] Persist on every activity/phase/state event and enrich the state file
description:
Files: packages/apra-fleet-workflow/src/viewer/index.mjs.

Hook the debounced writer to EVERY activity-end, phase, and state event -- the same events that already drive the SSE /events broadcast (see the broadcast call sites) -- rather than only run-end. Enrich the persisted object with: sprint id, launch args, verdict, PR URL, start/update/end timestamps, terminal reason.
acceptance_criteria: Every event that triggers an SSE broadcast also schedules a state write; the persisted JSON contains sprintId, args, verdict, prUrl, startedAt/updatedAt/endedAt, terminalReason; a mid-sprint read of the file shows in-progress state (not just terminal state); no SSE regression.
close_reason: Already implemented and tested in commit 08c8fe1a (viewer/index.mjs enriches state with sprintId/args/verdict/prUrl/timestamps/terminalReason, schedules debounced write in broadcast() on every event). Verified via apra-fleet-workflow-sprint-state.test.mjs (both tests pass).

title: [impl] Debounced sprint-state writer with flush-on-exit in viewer/index.mjs
description:
Files: packages/apra-fleet-workflow/src/viewer/index.mjs (persistState() around line 463, SIGINT/SIGTERM handlers ~503).

Replace the write-once-on-end persistState() with a debounced writer: coalesce bursts within a configurable window (default 300ms, must sit in the 200-500ms range), always flush synchronously before process exit (end, SIGINT, SIGTERM, and the existing stop path). Keep the existing sprint-logs/sprint_<HHMMSS>.json write exactly as-is -- it stays as the child crash-safety net; the new writer is additive and targets its own path (wired in a later task).
acceptance_criteria: A debounced writer coalesces N rapid state changes into 1 write within the window; flush-on-exit is synchronous and runs on end/SIGINT/SIGTERM/stop; the existing sprint-logs/ file content and naming are byte-for-byte unchanged (regression test); debounce window is configurable and defaults inside 200-500ms.
close_reason: Fixed the eft.2.3 concurrent-sprints test hang (listening-event race in withServer helper, plus closeAllConnections + non-zero test-timeout safety nets); removed 6 stray unrelated requirements.md scaffolding files per reviewer hygiene note. Full workflow package suite (143 tests) and root build pass clean.

title: [test] abort-PR paths: commits-exist, zero-commit, and PR idempotency
description:
Files: packages/apra-fleet-se/test/ (new mock-sprint-abort-pr.test.mjs, following the existing mock-sprint-finalization-*.test.mjs conventions and helpers/ mocks).

Cover: (a) a sprint that throws SprintPlanRejectedError with >=1 commit on the branch -> branch pushed and Auto-sprint [ABORTED] PR created with the error evidence in the body; (b) same abort with 0 commits -> no gh pr create call, but a terminal history record exists; (c) gh pr create returning 'already exists' -> swallowed, existing PR URL surfaced, no throw; (d) PASS/FAIL finalization unchanged (regression assert against existing golden expectations).
acceptance_criteria: New test file passes under npm test and fails if finalizeAbort() is reverted; all four cases (a)-(d) asserted explicitly; no reliance on network or a real gh binary (mocks only); test asserts the [ABORTED] PR title prefix exactly.
close_reason: mock-sprint-abort-pr.test.mjs covers all 4 cases (commits-exist -> pushed+[ABORTED] PR w/ error evidence, zero-commit -> no gh pr create but terminal history record, gh pr create 'already exists' swallowed, PASS/FAIL regression unaffected); no network/gh binary; verified passing.

title: [impl] Route typed sprint-abort errors through finalizeAbort in runner.js/cli.mjs; always write a terminal history record
description:
Files: packages/apra-fleet-se/auto-sprint/runner.js, packages/apra-fleet-se/bin/cli.mjs (catch block around line 594/637).

Catch the typed abort errors -- StalledSprintError, SprintPlanRejectedError, ReviewerContractViolationError, budget-exceeded, pre-sprint validation errors (all extend WorkflowError; see auto-sprint/errors.mjs) -- instead of letting them propagate uncaught to the cli.mjs top-level catch. On catch: call finalizeAbort(), then write a terminal history record (verdict ABORTED, terminal reason = error code, PR URL or null), then keep the existing grace window + exit 1 behavior. A zero-commit abort still writes the history record.
acceptance_criteria: Each typed abort error path calls finalizeAbort() and writes a terminal history record with verdict ABORTED and the error code as terminal reason; zero-commit aborts still write the history record with prUrl null; exit code stays 1 and the viewer grace window still runs; untyped/unexpected errors keep today's behavior.
close_reason: Verified existing commit 640a0ea6 already implements typed-abort routing through finalizeAbort() + terminal history record in runner.js's main(); cli.mjs's catch block at line ~594 is unchanged (grace window + exit 1 preserved) since finalizeAbort/history-record happen inside runner.js before the error propagates to engine.executeFile()'s caller. Full package test suite (347 tests) passes.

title: [impl] Add finalizeAbort() helper: commits-beyond-base check + [ABORTED] PR publish
description:
Files: packages/apra-fleet-se/auto-sprint/runner.js (near the existing PASS/FAIL finalization + sanitizePrText/computeBranchSlug helpers).

Add an exported finalizeAbort({ error, branch, baseBranch, member }) helper that: (1) counts commits on the sprint branch beyond base (git rev-list --count base..branch via command() with an explicit member_name); (2) if >=1 commit, pushes the branch and creates a PR titled 'Auto-sprint [ABORTED]: <goal/branch>' whose body carries the typed error evidence (error.code, message, details) run through sanitizePrText; (3) if 0 commits, creates NO PR and returns a result object saying so. PR creation must be idempotent: an 'already exists' failure from gh is swallowed and the existing PR URL returned.
acceptance_criteria: finalizeAbort() is exported from runner.js; with >=1 commit beyond base it pushes and opens an Auto-sprint [ABORTED] PR containing the error code and message; with 0 commits it opens no PR and returns { prUrl: null, reason: 'zero-commit-abort' }; a gh 'already exists' error is swallowed and the existing PR URL is returned; every command() call passes an explicit member_name; PASS/FAIL finalization code paths are untouched.
close_reason: Verified already implemented: finalizeAbort() exported in runner.js (line 2307), meets all acceptance criteria (commit-count gate, [ABORTED] PR with sanitized error evidence, zero-commit no-PR path, idempotent gh-already-exists handling, explicit member_name on every command() call). Confirmed via test/mock-sprint-abort-pr.test.mjs -- all 6 tests pass including PASS/FAIL regression checks proving those paths are untouched. No code change needed; this reopen (per the 2026-07-18 comment) was precautionary post-rebase verification.

title: [Phase 1] Combined member + issue-scope reservation ledger
description: Plan Part 2.2 + resolved Decisions (reservation authority; member economics). ONE combined ledger owned by the supervisor - reservations: { [sprintId]: { members, issueRoots, childPid, reservedAt } } - claiming both axes in exact lockstep (same launch event claims both, same terminal event releases both; one atomic write, one restart-reconciliation path). Member axis: POST /api/sprints atomically reserves the full member set (union of members + every roleMap value incl. orchestrator) all-or-nothing; overlap returns a conflict naming the other sprint. Issue-scope axis: identity key = root issue id(s) launched with; overlap detection RECOMPUTES the live-expanded subtree (reusing bdListScoped / bd list --parent, not reinvented) for every active sprint at launch-attempt time and intersects with the new request live-expanded scope - never a frozen launch-time snapshot (planners/reviewers grow the subtree mid-run). Any nonzero intersection rejects the ENTIRE launch, naming the conflicting sprint and overlapping bead ids (no partial-launch carve-out - that would require threading an exclude-set through the runner core loop; deferred). Ledger persisted to disk; on supervisor restart, PID-probe reconciliation releases both axes of dead-child reservations and marks the sprint aborted-by-restart. Manual force-release endpoint for wedged states. Known holes surfaced not hidden: (a) service-local ledger is bypassable by a manual bin/cli.mjs run - CONFIRMED acceptable for v1, server-side enforcement is the Phase 3 feature; (b) blocks-edges crossing two disjoint claimed scopes are not detected; (c) pre-Phase-2 the overlap check is best-effort against the service-local synced view - surface a scope-freshness (last synced N minutes ago) indicator. Confirmed member economics: whole-sprint member-per-role reservation even while idle; no role-level hand-off release in v1.
acceptance_criteria: Launching a sprint whose members or live-expanded issue scope overlap any active sprint returns a 409-style rejection naming the conflicting sprint id and the specific overlapping members/bead ids; both axes claim and release atomically on launch and on every terminal path (success, fail, abort, crash-reconciled); ledger survives supervisor restart and PID reconciliation releases dead-child reservations; force-release endpoint works; overlap check demonstrably uses live subtree recomputation (test: a child bead created after launch under a claimed root is still detected); scope-freshness indicator exposed via API; intra-sprint memberLocks behavior unchanged (orthogonal, both stay).
close_reason: Verified via 2026-07-20 real-bd integration run (node scripts/run-integ-suites.mjs, 0/74 failures, exitCode=0). Feature's [test] task's dedicated file(s) passed.

title: [Phase 1] Supervisor service: detached child-per-sprint, PID-liveness watchdog, HTTP API
description: Plan Part 2.1 (process model B - recommended and confirmed) + resolved Decision (service restart vs in-flight sprints). A small always-on supervisor process (fleet-se serve) owns the reservation ledger and an HTTP API, and spawns the existing bin/cli.mjs per sprint via spawn({detached: true, stdio: ignore}) with a per-sprint --viewer-port - true independently-surviving orphans; a crashed sprint cannot take down siblings or the service. Status detection per the confirmed design: same-instance exit events are authoritative, but supervisor RESTART is the severance point, so a dedicated liveness watchdog on a short interval PID-probes every ledger-listed sprint and combines with HTTP reachability into exactly four statuses: running-healthy (PID alive, HTTP answering), running-unresponsive (PID alive, HTTP not answering - operator attention, not auto-declared crash), crashed (PID gone, no terminal state persisted), finished (PID gone, terminal state in history). fork()/IPC explicitly rejected. Confirmed: detach-and-PID-readopt on restart is the target; sprints need NOT survive machine restart; journal/replay productization NOT required. HTTP surface: GET /api/members (list_members + reservation overlay), GET /api/backlog, POST /api/sprints (per-request goal - goal is already a per-invocation flag, just forwarded), GET /api/sprints and /api/sprints/:id (live proxied from child /state, else history), POST /api/sprints/:id/stop (proxies child /stop), POST /api/shutdown; service stops only via explicit shutdown/signal.
acceptance_criteria: Supervisor runs indefinitely and only exits on explicit shutdown; sprints launch as detached children of bin/cli.mjs with unique viewer ports; killing the supervisor leaves children running and a restarted supervisor re-adopts them by PID from the persisted ledger; the four statuses are distinguishable in the API and each is exercised by a test (healthy, hung child, killed child, completed child); all listed endpoints implemented with request validation reusing exported validateIssueId/validateBranchName/resolveRoleMap helpers; POST /api/sprints forwards per-request goal into child argv.
close_reason: Verified via 2026-07-20 real-bd integration run (node scripts/run-integ-suites.mjs, 0/74 failures, exitCode=0). Feature's [test] task's dedicated file(s) passed.

title: [Phase 0] Continuous per-activity sprint-state persistence (running/ -> old_sprints/)
description: Plan Part 2.3 (Continuous, per-activity persistence + Process-free history file layout) and Part 4 Phase 0. Today the viewer persists sprint state only on end/SIGINT/SIGTERM to sprint-logs/sprint_<HHMMSS>.json - a hard crash (SIGKILL, OOM) loses everything, and HHMMSS keys collide across days. Change: persist on EVERY activity-end, phase, and state event (the same events already driving the SSE broadcast). One file per sprint keyed by sprint id, overwritten in place: live sprint writes running/<sprintId>.json; on completion the file moves to old_sprints/<sprintId>.json, so is-this-sprint-live is a directory-membership check, not a stale field. State file enriched with sprint id, launch args, verdict, PR URL, timestamps, terminal reason. I/O cost mitigated with a debounced writer (coalesce bursts within 200-500ms, always flush before process exit). sprint-logs/ stays exactly as-is (child crash-safety net); old_sprints/ lives under the service data directory, not the repo checkout.
acceptance_criteria: State file is written on every activity/phase/state event via a debounced (200-500ms) writer with flush-on-exit; file is keyed by sprint id and contains args, verdict, PR URL, timestamps, terminal reason; live vs finished is determined by running/ vs old_sprints/ directory membership; a simulated hard-kill mid-sprint loses at most one debounce window of progress (test); existing sprint-logs/ behavior untouched.
close_reason: Verified via 2026-07-20 real-bd integration run (node scripts/run-integ-suites.mjs, 0/74 failures, exitCode=0). Feature's [test] task's dedicated file(s) passed.

title: [Phase 0] PR-on-abort: publish Auto-sprint [ABORTED] PR when the branch has commits
description: Plan Part 1.4 + Part 4 Phase 0 + resolved Decision (aborted sprint with zero commits). Today a PR is raised on both PASS and FAIL final verdicts, but sprints that end by THROWING (StalledSprintError, SprintPlanRejectedError, ReviewerContractViolationError, budget-exceeded, pre-sprint validation errors) propagate to the cli.mjs catch block: grace window, exit 1, no branch push, no PR. Fix: catch typed sprint-abort errors in runner.js/cli.mjs and, iff the branch has commits beyond base, push and raise a PR titled Auto-sprint [ABORTED]: <branch> with the failure evidence in the body. Confirmed policy: a zero-commit abort raises NO PR (empty-diff PR is noise) but a history record is always kept either way.
acceptance_criteria: Each typed abort error path, when the sprint branch has >=1 commit beyond base, pushes the branch and creates an idempotent Auto-sprint [ABORTED] PR whose body contains the error evidence; a zero-commit abort creates no PR but still writes a terminal history record; PASS/FAIL finalization behavior unchanged; unit tests cover commits-exist and zero-commit abort paths plus PR idempotency (already-exists swallowed).
close_reason: Verified via 2026-07-20 real-bd integration run (node scripts/run-integ-suites.mjs, 0/74 failures, exitCode=0). Feature's [test] task's dedicated file(s) passed.

title: Auto-sprint as a service: always-on multi-sprint supervisor
description:
Umbrella epic for the auto-sprint-as-a-service redesign of packages/apra-fleet-se/auto-sprint: turn the single-shot run-to-completion CLI sprint into an always-on supervisor service running N concurrent sprint child processes with member+issue-scope reservation, a sprint-stack dashboard, and orchestrator-bracketed git+dolt sync.

DESIGN PLAN (source of truth, consolidated single edition 2026-07-16): https://claude.ai/code/artifact/413dd5a5-68f1-4a46-b10f-992dff241d3f
Structure: Part 1 current-state findings (incl. clean dispatch-safety audit of runner.js, 1.6); Part 2 process model (spawn/detached/PID-liveness supervisor, combined member+issue-scope reservation ledger, sprint-stack dashboard, continuous persistence); Part 3 token-passing sync stance, orchestrator-bracketed sync authorship, Dolt conflict handling (verified PoC C.1-C.5, Path A/B recovery, Tier 0/1/2 conflict-escalation ladder for both Dolt and git); Part 4 phasing (Phase 0-3); Part 5 nine resolved architecture decisions.

BUILD ORDER: features carry a [Phase N] prefix per the plan Part 4. Phase 0 = standalone fixes with independent value; Phase 1 = service-ification (no dolt sync, topology guard untouched); Phase 2 = orchestrator-bracketed git+dolt sync (the risky 40 percent); Phase 3 = hardening (server-side reservation, vendored agent-contract updates, deferred crash-resume/merger-agent). Phases are ordering guidance, not hard blocks deps - only genuine prerequisites are noted in feature descriptions (per this repo GRAPH-SEMANTICS lesson: no artificial blocks edges).

CROSS-REFERENCE: apra-fleet-ire (Fleet installer: portable dolt CLI alongside bd) already exists as a standalone task and is a hard prerequisite/component of the Phase 2 Dolt-sync feature (Path A resolve-in-place runbook is unusable without a provisioned dolt binary). It is referenced from that feature, deliberately NOT re-created or reparented here.

Explicitly deferred by the plan (recorded, not scoped): multi-repo-per-project beads remotes; partial-launch scope carve-out; role-level reservation; machine-restart sprint resume; merger-agent/parallel doer streaks.
acceptance_criteria: All child feature beads closed or explicitly deferred with rationale; the service is the single supported user-facing entry point (Decision: CLI fate); Phases 0-1 shipped before Phase 2 sync work lands so new failure modes surface inside a service that can contain, display and record them (plan Part 4).

title: Evaluate codebase-memory-mcp vs Joern and document decision

1. Ease of integration (MCP stdio native vs JVM + custom REPL)
2. Dependency weight (single binary vs JVM + Scala)
3. Language breadth (158 vs ~10)
4. Analysis depth (structural graph vs CPG with data flow)
5. MCP tool coverage (15 tools mapped to 7 provider methods)

Record the decision in the source code. Update code-intelligence-joern.ts header to note the evaluation outcome. The comparison data is already available in the requirements doc; this task is about documenting the decision in a durable, discoverable location within the codebase.

Acceptance criteria:
- A comparison section exists in the codebase covering all 5 dimensions
- The decision (codebase-memory-mcp selected or not) is clearly stated with rationale
- Joern file updated to reflect the evaluation outcome

Joern requires a JVM and Scala REPL. If DeusData is viable, implement CodebaseMemoryProvider following the same pattern as GitNexusProvider, register it in PROVIDERS, and make it the default.
