title: [integ] Setup's documented clone step leaves git origin pointing at real fleet-e2e-toy, permanently failing check-sandbox-sync-remote.mjs's 4th check
description:
Feature: N/A (playbook part 2 -- smoke test sandbox safety, integ-test-playbook.md ## Setup + its Neutralize/Verify subsection)
Expected: integ-test-playbook.md ## Setup's Verify step says scripts/check-sandbox-sync-remote.mjs 'exits 0 (OK lines) when all three hold' after the documented neutralize sequence (YAML sed patch + bd dolt remote remove). Running it after those exact documented steps should report all-clean, since the playbook frames this as the gate before proceeding to any auto-sprint run.
Actual: the script (extended by apra-fleet-eft.31, closed) now runs a 4th check, checkGitOriginNotHazard, that asserts the sandbox clone's OWN 'git remote get-url origin' does not point at the hazard remote. But integ-test-playbook.md ## Setup's own documented step is 'git clone https://github.com/Apra-Labs/fleet-e2e-toy "$HOME/toy-repo"' -- which by definition sets git origin to that exact URL -- and neither ## Setup nor the Neutralize subsection includes any step to rewrite/unset the clone's git origin. Consequently checkGitOriginNotHazard FAILS every single time immediately after following the playbook's Setup steps verbatim, with no documented remediation in the playbook to make it pass. Reproduced this cycle: after 'bd bootstrap --yes' + the YAML sed patch + 'bd dolt remote remove origin' (all three original checks report OK), the script still exits 1 solely on check 4: 'FAIL: git origin remote in .../toy-repo is https://github.com/Apra-Labs/fleet-e2e-toy -- points at fleet-e2e-toy, so any future bd dolt invocation that auto-provisions a Dolt remote from git origin (apra-fleet-eft.30/eft.35) would re-wire straight back to the real remote even if the checks above currently report clean.'
Assessed current risk: LOW in practice right now -- apra-fleet-eft.35 (closed) already made doltPullBefore()/doltPushAfter() fail-closed on isMemberSyncRemoteConfigured (the bd-level sync.remote key, confirmed inert this run via check 1), so no 'bd dolt pull/push' should fire at all in this sandbox regardless of what git origin points to. Check 4 is a defense-in-depth guard against a hypothetical future regression re-opening a path that bypasses that pre-gate, not a currently-live exploit. Proceeded with this cycle's smoke test on that basis, watching for any 'Configured Dolt remote origin from git origin' or live push log line as a signal to treat as an eft.30/35 recurrence.
Test: node scripts/check-sandbox-sync-remote.mjs "$HOME/toy-repo" immediately after the documented ## Setup + Neutralize sequence -- exits 1, only the 4th line is FAIL, other three are OK.
Repro: 1) Fresh sandbox per playbook ## Setup (install, start, git clone fleet-e2e-toy). 2) bd bootstrap --yes in $HOME/toy-repo. 3) Run the documented sed YAML patch + bd dolt remote remove origin. 4) node scripts/check-sandbox-sync-remote.mjs "$HOME/toy-repo" -- exits 1 on checkGitOriginNotHazard every time, since step 1's git clone is what set origin in the first place and no step ever changes it back.
Fix direction: either (a) add a documented ## Setup or Neutralize step that rewrites/removes the sandbox clone's git origin (e.g. 'git remote set-url origin file:///dev/null' or 'git remote remove origin') and re-run the verify script after it, or (b) relax checkGitOriginNotHazard to treat a clone's own origin as expected/inert once checks 1-3 hold (its purpose per apra-fleet-eft.31 is catching a re-provisioned Dolt remote deriving from origin, not the git origin's mere existence).
close_reason: integ-test-runner (2026-07-21): no longer reproduces. Ran node scripts/check-sandbox-sync-remote.mjs $HOME/toy-repo immediately after this cycle's full documented ## Setup Neutralize sequence (YAML sed patch, bd dolt remote remove, git remote set-url origin file:///dev/null/neutralized-sandbox-origin per eft.31): all 4 checks report OK, including 'git origin remote ... does not reference fleet-e2e-toy'. Note: the origin-neutralize step itself now breaks the sprint engine's own 'git fetch origin' -- filed separately as apra-fleet-eft.47, a distinct downstream consequence, not this bug's verify-script defect.

title: Surface GET /activities/:id/output as a 'more...' button on the activity widget (eft.27.4 addendum)
description: eft.27.4 added the on-demand full-output endpoint GET /activities/:id/output (command-output-cap.mjs / viewer index.mjs) plus an inline marker in the capped output, but the dashboard client template never fetches it, so a user reading a truncated command activity has no way to load the full text. Per eft.27.4's USER FEEDBACK addendum: add a 'more...' button on the individual activity widget ONLY (one item expanded at a time; do NOT make headers/titles expandable) that fetches /activities/:id/output on click for a capped command activity (outputTruncated/errorTruncated), without regressing the lean viewer's snappiness. Consider unifying with the still-unsurfaced eft.27.2 /beads/:id/description endpoint under parent eft.27.
notes:
REOPENED (2026-07-21, user-directed): post-closure live verification on the
run-19 dashboard shows the button renders NOWHERE. /state has 19 activities
with outputTruncated:true yet the DOM contains 0 more... buttons. Root
cause: real capped command activities ship NO inline text field at all
(sample keys: outputTruncated, outputByteLength=1421947, summary -- no
'output'), and fieldBlock only attaches the button while rendering the text
field. The closing unit test fabricated an activity with BOTH output and
outputTruncated -- a shape the real pipeline never produces.
FIX: key the more... control on the markers (<field>Truncated /
<field>ByteLength) independent of inline text presence; absent field =>
render summary/empty preview + button; full text via GET
/activities/:id/output on demand. EXTEND SCOPE to agent/LLM dispatch
activities (prompt AND response fields) -- the original user ask ('no way to
view the output of an LLM call'); live evidence: planner Response renders
truncated mid-word with no control. Keep the one-expanded-at-a-time DOM
discipline. ACCEPTANCE: (1) DOM test with a REAL-pipeline-shaped activity
(markers, no inline text) renders the button and expands via the endpoint;
(2) HTTP check integ can run: /activities/<id>/output serves full text for
a live capped activity (command AND agent).
CLAIMED by orchestrator (2026-07-21): implementing directly in a side worktree; DO NOT DISPATCH to doers this cycle -- commits will land on the sprint branch shortly. Stays OPEN after impl for reviewer/integ verification.
close_reason: Re-implemented at 0f37266: more... keyed on <field>Truncated/<field>ByteLength markers (real pipeline shape -- no inline text required; summary as preview), agent/LLM activities get markers via lean-state summarizeHeavyFields, /activities/:id/output falls back to live state.tree so full agent responses (already retained server-side) are served. Test file rewritten against REAL shapes incl. HTTP round-trip + one-expanded-at-a-time. 213/213 green. Reviewer: verify on next cycle per eft.44-consolidated acceptance (HTTP check integ can run).

title: [test] stored command-activity output is capped and the recurring /state poll payload stays under 1 MB even with large bd-list command output
description:
What to test: drive a sprint (or synthesize activity state) that runs a command emitting >1 MB of stdout (e.g. a large 'bd list --all --json' dump). Assert (1) no single stored command-activity event in sprint state exceeds the configured cap; (2) the recurring /state poll payload stays under 1 MB regardless of how chatty the captured command was; (3) the on-demand full-output endpoint returns the complete original output for a given activity id.

How to assert: unit/integration test in packages/apra-fleet-se mirroring the eft.27.3 perf harness.

Acceptance criteria: (1) test fails against pre-fix behaviour (full multi-MB dump stored in state); (2) passes after eft.27.4; (3) verifies both the in-state cap and the on-demand full-output retrieval path.
notes: USER FEEDBACK addendum: also assert the UI contract -- full detail is reachable via a per-activity 'more...' control fetching on demand, and the recurring poll payload stays lean (snappiness is the top acceptance criterion). See parent eft.27 notes.
close_reason: Added packages/apra-fleet-workflow/test/apra-fleet-workflow-command-output-cap-perf.test.mjs mirroring the eft.27.3 perf harness at eft.27's measured incident scale (60 x ~1.3MB command activities + a 6MB single dump): verifies the persisted running-state cap, sub-1MB recurring GET /state poll payload, and byte-for-byte on-demand output retrieval. Full apra-fleet-workflow suite green (197 pass).

title: [impl] Cap captured command-activity stdout stored in sprint state (head+tail excerpt + byte count; full output on-demand)
description:
Context: eft.27 MEASURED ROOT CAUSE (live 188 MB /state payload dissected): 130 command-activity events at ~1.3 MB each (164 MB of 188 MB total) each store the full captured stdout of 'bd list --all --limit 0 --json'. The closed children (eft.27.1 lean list-state endpoint, eft.27.2 on-demand description, eft.27.3 perf test) do NOT cap this captured command output -- it is the largest remaining byte source per the NOTES refinement.

Files likely involved: the engine/viewer activity-state capture in packages/apra-fleet-se where a command activity's stdout is written into sprint state, plus an on-demand full-output endpoint.

Expected behaviour: when storing a command activity's captured stdout into sprint state, keep only a bounded head excerpt + tail excerpt + total byte count (configurable cap), not the full output, regardless of which command is chatty. Expose the full captured output via an on-demand endpoint keyed by activity id so the viewer can fetch it only when a user expands that activity.

Acceptance criteria: (1) no single stored command-activity event exceeds the configured cap; (2) the /state payload no longer carries multi-MB command dumps; (3) full original output remains retrievable on demand by activity id; (4) behaviour is command-agnostic (any chatty command is capped, not just bd list).
notes: USER FEEDBACK addendum: the on-demand full-output endpoint this task adds must be surfaced in the viewer as a 'more...' button on the individual activity widget ONLY (one item expanded at a time); do NOT make headers/titles expandable; must not regress the lean viewer's snappiness. See parent eft.27 notes.
close_reason: Capped command-activity stdout/error stored in sprint state to a configurable head+tail excerpt + byte count, added GET /activities/:id/output on-demand endpoint, 10 new tests, full suite green (191 pass).

title: Add fail-closed unit coverage for dolt-sync benign-skip branch
description: eft.30.2 added isMemberSyncRemoteConfigured plus a doltPushAfter benign-skip branch and exposed a checkSyncRemoteConfigured override hook for tests, but the safety-critical fail-closed default is only manually verified. Add coverage in the dolt-sync test suite for: (a) a non-diverged push failure with bd-level sync.remote neutralized or absent returns ok:true, skipped:true, reason no-remote and does NOT throw DoltSyncError; (b) the same failure with an actively configured sync.remote still throws DoltSyncError (eft.16.1 no-regression); (c) fail-closed paths -- command() throwing, failSoft error result, unparseable output, and empty output all treat the member as configured and still throw. Reference: packages/apra-fleet-se/test/dolt-sync-brackets.test.mjs.
close_reason: Closed

title: [test] viewer perf/regression on a 500+ activity sprint: summary-only list shape, poll payload <1MB, expand-to-paint <200ms, cache hit on repeat expand
description:
Verifies the fixes from apra-fleet-eft.27.1 and .27.2 against the acceptance criteria of apra-fleet-eft.27.

What to test (against a fixture sprint with 500+ activities):
1. List-state endpoint is pinned to summary-only shape: assert NO description/transcript fields present (regression guard).
2. Recurring poll payload under 1 MB regardless of sprint length.
3. Expand-click-to-paint under 200 ms; no main-thread long task over 500 ms during steady-state polling (PerformanceObserver longtask evidence where the harness supports it, otherwise assert handler+render timing bound).
4. Descriptions load on demand: first expand fetches; a second expand of an unchanged bead causes NO network request (localStorage cache hit keyed by id, validated by updatedAt); a refetch occurs after the bead's updatedAt changes.
5. No page-is-unresponsive dialog across a 50-expand-click session (or the closest deterministic proxy the test harness allows).

How to assert pass/fail:
- node --test / vitest per convention for the endpoint-shape and payload-size assertions; a headless/browser-style or DOM-harness test for expand timing + cache behaviour where feasible, otherwise unit-level assertions on the client fetch/cache module.

Done criteria:
- All checks pass after eft.27.1 + eft.27.2; the summary-only shape assertion fails against the pre-fix full-payload endpoint.
- Prefix [test]; npm test passes; ASCII-only.
close_reason: Closed

title: [impl] On-demand description endpoint (GET /beads/:id/description) + browser localStorage cache keyed by bead id, validated by updatedAt
description:
Files: viewer server route (GET /beads/:id/description or equivalent) in packages/apra-fleet-workflow, and the served page's expand handler / client fetch+cache logic.

Problem (from apra-fleet-eft.27): descriptions/transcripts must load on demand (90 percent of use cases never open them), and repeat views must not re-fetch/re-parse.

Behaviour to implement:
- Add an on-demand endpoint (GET /beads/:id/description or equivalent) that returns a single bead's full description/transcript, fetched only when the user expands.
- Browser-side cache in localStorage keyed by bead id, validated against the updatedAt/last-modified timestamp carried in the lean list state (from eft.27.1): read cache first, refetch only when the timestamp changed. Append-only payloads (agent transcripts) may use content-hash keys.
- If any large parse remains after this, fetch+parse in a Web Worker so the main thread never blocks.

Depends on eft.27.1 (needs the lean list state's updatedAt timestamp for cache validation).

Done criteria:
- Expand-click-to-paint under 200 ms; no main-thread long task over 500 ms during steady-state polling.
- A second expand of an unchanged bead causes NO network request (cache hit); a refetch occurs after the bead's updatedAt changes.
- No page-is-unresponsive dialog across a 50-expand-click session.
- npm test passes; ASCII-only.
close_reason: Closed

title: [impl] Lean list-state /state endpoint: carry only id/title/updatedAt/short-summary; never embed descriptions/transcripts; server-side dedup remaining repeats
description:
Files: the viewer/dashboard state endpoint and serializer in packages/apra-fleet-workflow (createDashboardViewer / GET /state serialization) plus the served page's list-render path.

Problem (from apra-fleet-eft.27): GET /state returns a 116 MB payload (85 MB pure duplication) per poll; parsing+rendering it blocks the main thread (49634 ms long task measured). Existing poll-coalescing/render-once guards reduce frequency, not per-render O(entire-history) cost.

Behaviour to implement:
- Lean list state: activities and referenced beads carry only id, title, updatedAt, and short summaries. Descriptions and agent transcripts are NEVER embedded in list state.
- Server-side dedup for any remaining repeated strings: send each unique string once with references (append-only payloads like transcripts may use content-hash keys).
- Keep the endpoint target-agnostic and sprint-size-agnostic.

Done criteria:
- Recurring poll payload is under 1 MB regardless of sprint length (measured against a 500+ activity sprint fixture).
- List-state shape contains no description/transcript fields.
- npm test passes; ASCII-only.
close_reason: Implemented lean list-state /state endpoint: src/viewer/lean-state.mjs strips description/output/error/input/transcript/stdout/stderr into short summaries and dedupes repeated strings via a shared reference table; GET /state now uses buildListStatePayload(). 500-activity fixture stays under 1MB (regression test). npm test passes across apra-fleet-workflow (177), apra-fleet-se (910), and root vitest (2379).

title: [test] workflow/cli-launched sprint reserves members server-side; overlapping POST /api/sprints returns 409; release verified on abnormal exit
description:
Verifies the fixes from apra-fleet-eft.26.1 and .26.2. Covers the acceptance criteria of apra-fleet-eft.26.

What to test:
1. Reserve/release bracket: a sprint launched via runner.js/bin/cli.mjs reserves each 'members' entry server-side at startup and releases on every exit path (success, stall-abort, SIGINT). Assert reserved during run, released after each exit path.
2. Cross-sprint overlap (ledger-empty-but-server-reserved): with such a sprint holding member M and the supervisor ledger empty, POST /api/sprints whose member union includes M returns 409 naming the owning sprint id.
3. Overlay: GET /api/members shows the server-side reservation for M.
4. Abnormal-exit release: release on a killed child is verified via lease expiry or watchdog force-release; document the mechanism in the test/notes.

How to assert pass/fail:
- Unit/integration tests in the supervisor + auto-sprint suites (node --test / vitest per convention), using the fleet server test harness and a stubbed member. Assert HTTP 409 body names the owning sprint id; assert list_members reservedBy transitions.

Done criteria:
- All four checks pass after eft.26.1 + eft.26.2; fail against pre-fix behaviour.
- Prefix [test]; npm test passes; ASCII-only.
close_reason: Added packages/apra-fleet-se/test/reservation-interop-e2e.test.mjs: end-to-end coverage composing eft.26.1's reserve/release bracket + eft.26.2's overlap guard/overlay against a fake fleet-server registry mirroring member-reservation.ts semantics. Covers all 4 acceptance cases (reserve/release on success/stall-abort/SIGINT; ledger-empty-but-server-reserved 409 naming the owning sprint; /api/members overlay; abnormal-exit release documented as no-lease-expiry + operator-driven force_release). 10 new tests pass; full apra-fleet-se suite (934 tests) green.

title: [impl] defaultMemberOverlapGuard reads server-side reservedBy and rejects overlapping launch with 409; /api/members overlay shows server-side reservations
description:
Files: packages/apra-fleet-se/src/supervisor/api.mjs (defaultMemberOverlapGuard, /api/sprints launch validation, /api/members overlay).

Problem (from apra-fleet-eft.26, Hole 2): defaultMemberOverlapGuard consults only its own ledger.list(), never the fleet server reservedBy field, so a reservation made by other means (e.g. a workflow-launched sprint after eft.26.1) is not seen at launch validation. Net effect: POST /api/sprints including an already-reserved member M is accepted (no 409) and two sprints interleave on M.

Behaviour to implement:
- defaultMemberOverlapGuard additionally reads server member records (list_members reservedBy) and rejects a launch that overlaps ANY server-side reservation, returning 409 and naming the owning sprint id.
- The supervisor /api/members reservation overlay surfaces server-side reservations (not just ledger entries) so GET /api/members shows reservation for M.
- Stay target-agnostic; sprint id is opaque.

Done criteria:
- With member M reserved server-side (ledger empty), POST /api/sprints whose member union includes M returns 409 naming the owning sprint id.
- GET /api/members shows the server-side reservation for M.
- npm test passes; ASCII-only.
close_reason: defaultMemberOverlapGuard now consults server-side reservedBy (via listMembers) merged with local-ledger conflicts, rejecting overlapping launches 409 naming the owning sprint id; GET /api/members overlay falls back to server-side reservedBy when the local ledger has no reservation. 11 new/updated unit tests pass; full apra-fleet-se suite (751 tests) green.

title: [impl] runner.js reserves each sprint member server-side at sprint start and releases on every exit path (success/stall-abort/SIGINT)
description:
Files: packages/apra-fleet-se/auto-sprint/runner.js (and bin/cli.mjs entry as needed).

Problem (from apra-fleet-eft.26, Hole 1): a sprint launched directly via 'apra-fleet workflow auto-sprint' (or bin/cli.mjs) never reserves its members server-side -- neither cli.mjs nor runner.js calls member_reservation at sprint start or release at sprint end (the existing reserve/release logic there is only the bead id-allocator, unrelated). So a workflow-launched sprint is invisible to the supervisor and two sprints can interleave dispatches on the same member.

Behaviour to implement:
- At sprint start, runner.js reserves every 'members' entry server-side via member_reservation, using sprint id = the same opaque identity already used for the dolt-mutex/allocator.
- Release every reserved member on EVERY exit path: normal success, stall-abort, and SIGINT (finally-block / signal handler). Keep sprint id opaque and target-agnostic (no assumptions about which repo the sprint develops).

Done criteria:
- Members are reserved at startup and released on success, stall-abort, and SIGINT.
- Reserve/release bracket is covered by unit tests (may live in the eft.26 test task).
- npm test passes; ASCII-only.
close_reason: Implemented createMemberReservationClient in runner.js + reserve/release bracket in cli.mjs (success/catch-stall-abort/SIGINT); 9 new unit tests pass; npm test suite green.

title: Viewer unusable on large sprints: full-state polling blocks the main thread for tens of seconds
description: Measured first-hand against a live 449-activity sprint (viewer on :18300, about 3h uptime, Chrome). GET /state returns a 116 MB payload per poll (2.8s transfer); the page then JSON-parses and re-renders it on the main thread. PerformanceObserver longtask (buffered) recorded a 49634 ms long task plus a 4199 ms one; an expand click measured 0 ms in the handler but 9009 ms click-to-paint; steady-state 250 ms interval drift spikes to about 750 ms. User-visible effect: Chrome page-is-unresponsive dialog every 2-3 expand clicks -- the viewer is effectively unusable for large sprints. Payload anatomy: 679 strings of 200+ chars total 110 MB, of which only 25 MB is unique -- 85 MB (73 percent) is pure duplication, e.g. one 202-char string repeated 63 times: the same bead descriptions and command outputs are embedded again and again by every bd command activity. The existing mitigations (400 ms poll coalescing, data-rendered render-once guard, flex min-height scroll chain) are confirmed present in the served page and insufficient: they reduce render frequency, not the O(entire-history) cost of each render. Fix direction (user-specified): (1) lean list state -- activities and referenced beads carry only id, title, updatedAt and short summaries; descriptions and transcripts are NEVER embedded in list state (90 percent of use cases never open them); (2) on-demand description endpoint (GET /beads/:id/description or equivalent) fetched only when the user expands; (3) browser-side cache in localStorage keyed by bead id, validated with the last-modified/updatedAt timestamp carried in the lean list state -- dip into the cache first, refetch only when the timestamp changed; append-only payloads like agent transcripts may use content-hash keys; (4) server-side dedup for any remaining repeated strings (send each unique string once with references); (5) if any large parse remains, fetch+parse in a Web Worker. Keep everything target-agnostic and sprint-size-agnostic.
acceptance_criteria: Against a sprint with 500+ activities: 1. expand-click-to-paint under 200 ms; 2. no main-thread long task over 500 ms during steady-state polling (PerformanceObserver longtask evidence in the closing note); 3. recurring poll payload under 1 MB regardless of sprint length; 4. descriptions load on demand and repeat views hit the localStorage cache (verified by a second expand causing no network request when updatedAt is unchanged, and a refetch after the bead is updated); 5. no page-is-unresponsive dialog across a 50-expand-click session; 6. regression test pins the list-state endpoint to summary-only shape (no description/transcript fields).
notes:
MEASURED ROOT CAUSE REFINEMENT (live 188 MB /state payload dissected in-browser): the median activity event is 1 KB -- normal. The bloat is concentrated in 130 command-activity events of about 1.3 MB EACH (164 MB of the 188 MB total): every one is the complete captured stdout of bd list --all --limit 0 --json (a full project-wide beads dump with all descriptions and acceptance criteria), which the engine runs repeatedly in its scoping/sync brackets and the viewer stores verbatim in state. Prioritize accordingly: (a) cap captured command output stored in activity state -- keep a head plus tail excerpt and total byte count, with the full output available only via an on-demand endpoint -- this removes the 164 MB structurally regardless of which command is chatty; (b) generic string dedup helps less than expected here because successive dumps differ slightly as beads change; (c) the related engine-side waste of running bd list --all --limit 0 repeatedly is tracked as apra-fleet-eft.15 -- fixing the query frequency there compounds with the output cap here. The lean list-state work (eft.27.1) and localStorage/on-demand description work remain valid; this note redirects the largest remaining byte source.
USER FEEDBACK (2026-07-20, live on the eft.27.1 lean viewer during run 16): (1) KEEP THE SNAPPINESS -- the lightweight fast experience of the lean viewer is loved and is the top constraint; no fix may regress it. (2) Detail viewing gap: there is currently NO way to view full descriptions / LLM call output in the viewer. Required UX: ONLY the lowest-level widget (an individual activity item) gets a 'more...' button that loads FULL detail for that ONE item on demand (via the on-demand endpoints); header/title ellipses must NOT be expandable -- truncated titles stay truncated. One item expanded at a time. (3) Scroll problem NOT fixed: the scrollability crisis is still present in the lean viewer and still needs a real fix.
MINOR BUG (user-observed via Save button, 2026-07-20): the viewer's Save/download exports globalState which still carries the top-level _strings dedup table (114 entries in the observed file) even though every $ref was already resolved inline -- the table is pure duplicated dead weight in the saved workflow-state.json and misleads readers into thinking the file is interned. Fix: strip _strings from the resolved state after resolveStringRefs() in poll() (or exclude it in saveState()) in packages/apra-fleet-workflow/src/viewer/index.mjs.
close_reason: Verified 2026-07-20/21: viewer-perf-regression-e2e.test.mjs (eft.27.3, 500+ activity fixture) passed clean in playbook part 1's 85/85 real-bd suite. apra-fleet-workflow-command-output-cap-perf.test.mjs (eft.27.4/27.5) run directly this cycle: all 6 tests pass.

title: Reservation interop gap: workflow/cli-launched sprints are invisible to the supervisor
description: Server-side member reservation (eft.10.1-.3) is enforced one-directionally. Direction A works: a supervisor-launched sprint reserves its members (ledger drives server reserve/release via member_reservation), and execute-prompt.ts rejects any dispatch to a member whose reservedBy names a different sprint (currentSprintId from APRA_FLEET_SPRINT_ID stamped by the supervisor spawner into the per-sprint server env), so a workflow-launched sprint (currentSprintId undefined) cannot dispatch to a supervisor-reserved member. Direction B is a gap with two holes. Hole 1: a sprint launched directly via apra-fleet workflow auto-sprint (or bin/cli.mjs) never reserves its members server-side -- neither cli.mjs nor auto-sprint/runner.js calls member_reservation at sprint start or release at sprint end (the only reserve/release logic there is the bead id-allocator, unrelated). Hole 2: the supervisor launch overlap guard (defaultMemberOverlapGuard in packages/apra-fleet-se/src/supervisor/api.mjs) consults only its own ledger.list(), never the fleet server reservedBy field, so even a reservation made by other means is not seen at launch validation. Net effect: while a workflow-launched sprint runs on member M, POST /api/sprints with members including M is accepted (no 409) and two sprints interleave dispatches on M -- the unsafe concurrent-sprint case apra-fleet-qv1 describes; the per-member busy-lock only prevents moment-to-moment dispatch overlap, not cross-sprint interleaving. Fix direction: (1) runner.js reserves every sprint member server-side at sprint start (sprint id = the same identity used for the dolt-mutex/allocator) and releases in the finally path including abort; (2) defaultMemberOverlapGuard additionally reads the server member records (list_members reservedBy) and rejects a launch overlapping ANY server-side reservation, naming the owning sprint id; (3) the supervisor /api/members reservation overlay shows server-side reservations too. All surfaces stay target-agnostic: sprint id is opaque, no assumptions about which repo the sprint develops.
acceptance_criteria: 1. A sprint launched via apra-fleet workflow auto-sprint (and via bin/cli.mjs directly) reserves each members entry server-side at startup and releases on every exit path (success, stall-abort, SIGINT); unit tests cover the reserve/release bracket. 2. With such a sprint holding member M, POST /api/sprints whose member union includes M returns 409 naming the owning sprint id; unit test covers ledger-empty-but-server-reserved. 3. GET /api/members shows the server-side reservation for M. 4. Release on abnormal exit (killed child) is verified via lease expiry or watchdog force-release and documented.
close_reason: Verified 2026-07-20/21: reservation-interop-e2e.test.mjs (eft.26.3, exercising the eft.26.1+eft.26.2 fix pair end to end) passed clean in playbook part 1's 85/85 real-bd suite.

title: (BUG) First-run onboarding banner corrupts JSON tool responses on the very first MCP call after fresh install
description:
Discovered during apra-fleet-eft.22 (undici bundling re-verification) end-to-end smoke of a fresh dev-mode install.

Repro: fresh sandbox HOME, 'node dist/index.js install' + 'start', 'register-member' via the CLI (src/cli/register-member.ts calls tools/register-member.ts directly, bypassing the MCP tool-registry wrapTool() onboarding path entirely), then run 'node dist/index.js workflow auto-sprint ...'. auto-sprint's cli.mjs (packages/apra-fleet-se/bin/cli.mjs ~line 524) calls fleetApi.listMembers({format:'json'}) as its first-ever MCP tool call against this server session, then does JSON.parse(listRes.content[0].text) assuming content[0] is the raw JSON payload.

Root cause: src/services/tool-registry.ts wrapTool()'s getOnboardingPreamble() (line ~59) calls getFirstRunPreamble() and returns the banner UNCONDITIONALLY when !bannerShown, before checking isJson -- see the isJson check only guards getWelcomeBackPreamble(), not the first-run banner. So on a truly fresh server, the first MCP tool call to ANY active tool (including JSON-typed ones like list_members) gets a content[0] block of '<apra-fleet-display>...banner...</apra-fleet-display>' prepended ahead of the actual JSON text, breaking any machine caller that assumes content[0] is the JSON payload. Client-visible symptom: 'Error: Failed to list fleet members: Unexpected token '<', "<apra-flee"... is not valid JSON', and the sprint aborts before Sprint Setup.

Impact: because register-member is CLI-only and bypasses the banner-consuming tool-registry path, list_members via auto-sprint's cli.mjs is typically the literal first MCP tool call after a fresh install in the real 'install -> register-member -> auto-sprint' flow, so this reproduces on essentially every very first auto-sprint launch on a fresh install (bannerShown is persisted after the first call, so retries/subsequent runs do not repro).

Fix direction: getOnboardingPreamble() should only return the first-run banner for non-JSON-response tool calls (move the isJson check above the getFirstRunPreamble() call, matching the existing isJson guard on getWelcomeBackPreamble()), OR cli.mjs's JSON-consuming call sites should scan content[] for the block without an <apra-fleet-display> wrapper instead of assuming content[0].

Not part of apra-fleet-eft.22/eft.19 scope (those are specifically about undici module resolution, which is independently confirmed fixed -- see eft.22 close notes). Filing separately per the acceptance-criteria instruction to report concrete issues found without folding them into the undici verification.
close_reason: Fixed: moved isJson check above getFirstRunPreamble() in getOnboardingPreamble() (src/services/tool-registry.ts), matching the existing getWelcomeBackPreamble() guard. Banner is now deferred (not lost) until the next non-JSON active-tool call, so a JSON-typed tool's content[0] is never corrupted on the first call after a fresh install. Updated tests/onboarding.test.ts and tests/onboarding-smoke.mjs which had encoded the old JSON-bypass behavior (from commit 75d36972 / PR #101) as expected; added a regression test asserting a JSON-typed tool's first-ever call on a fresh server returns parseable, unprefixed content[0]. Full vitest suite: 2361 passed / 12 skipped. Build clean.

title: [test] Teardown block actually removes the sandbox after HOME override (tilde-resolution regression guard)
description: Add a shell/vitest regression test that guards the tilde-after-HOME-reassignment defect for the integ-test-playbook.md Teardown pattern. In an isolated temp dir, reproduce the pattern: set HOME to a sandbox path, create $HOME/temp/.apra-fleet-tests with a marker file, then run the CORRECTED teardown sequence (capture absolute sandbox path before reassigning HOME, then rm -rf the captured path) and assert the sandbox directory and marker are actually gone. Also assert the buggy form (bare tilde resolved after HOME override) would NOT remove it -- i.e. the test demonstrates the fix. Do not touch the real user HOME; operate entirely within a mktemp sandbox. Use the repo test framework where practical.
acceptance_criteria: A test reproduces the HOME-override teardown pattern in an isolated temp sandbox and asserts the corrected sequence removes the sandbox directory (marker file gone), while the pre-fix bare-tilde form leaves it intact. Test never touches the real user home directory and passes deterministically.
close_reason: Added tests/integ-test-playbook-teardown.test.ts reproducing the Teardown HOME-override pattern in a mktemp sandbox; verifies corrected SANDBOX-capture form removes the sandbox+marker and the pre-fix bare-tilde form leaves it intact.

title: [impl] Fix integ-test-playbook.md Teardown tilde no-op: capture absolute sandbox path before reassigning HOME
description: In integ-test-playbook.md the Teardown block (around lines 153-157) exports HOME=~/temp/.apra-fleet-tests and then runs rm -rf ~/temp/.apra-fleet-tests. Because HOME was reassigned first, the trailing tilde now expands against the OVERRIDDEN HOME, so rm targets $HOME/temp/.apra-fleet-tests (i.e. .../temp/.apra-fleet-tests/temp/.apra-fleet-tests), a nonexistent path -- rm -rf exits 0 silently and the real sandbox is never deleted. Fix: capture the absolute sandbox path into a variable BEFORE any HOME reassignment and use it for deletion, e.g. as the first line of each block: SANDBOX="$HOME/temp/.apra-fleet-tests" (using the real pre-override HOME), then rm -rf "$SANDBOX" in Teardown. Audit the whole file: every block that both exports HOME and later references a tilde path (Setup ~line 83-90, Test scenario ~line 103-106, Teardown ~line 153-157) must resolve sandbox paths via the pre-captured absolute variable rather than a tilde that resolves post-reassignment. Only edit integ-test-playbook.md.
acceptance_criteria: integ-test-playbook.md Teardown deletes the actual sandbox: it captures the absolute sandbox path before overriding HOME and rm -rf targets that captured path (no bare tilde resolved after HOME reassignment). Any other block that exports HOME and later uses a tilde-relative sandbox path is likewise corrected. ASCII-only, no non-ASCII characters introduced.
close_reason: Closed

title: [integ] integ-test-playbook.md Teardown: 'rm -rf ~/temp/.apra-fleet-tests' silently no-ops because HOME was already overridden to that same path earlier in the block
description:
Feature: integ-test-playbook.md ## Teardown section (not a product feature -- filing as [integ] since it is a real, reproducible defect discovered while running the playbook)
Expected: Teardown 'stops the server and deletes the sandbox entirely, so no state accumulates or drifts from a fresh install between runs' (playbook's own stated purpose).
Actual: The Teardown block is:
  export HOME=~/temp/.apra-fleet-tests
  export USERPROFILE="$HOME"
  export APRA_FLEET_PORT=18700
  node dist/index.js stop
  rm -rf ~/temp/.apra-fleet-tests
Because HOME is reassigned to the sandbox path on line 1, bash's tilde expansion on the LAST line now resolves '~' using the NEW (already-overridden) $HOME, not the real user home. So 'rm -rf ~/temp/.apra-fleet-tests' actually targets $REAL_HOME/temp/.apra-fleet-tests/temp/.apra-fleet-tests, which does not exist. rm -rf on a nonexistent path exits 0 and prints nothing, so the failure is completely silent -- the command 'succeeds' while leaving the real sandbox directory ($REAL_HOME/temp/.apra-fleet-tests) fully intact on disk. Same class of issue would also affect USERPROFILE-based tilde expansion on Windows if PowerShell/cmd similarly re-resolved after the reassignment (not verified on this run -- this run is macOS/zsh).
Test: Ran integ-test-playbook.md Setup, Test scenario, and Teardown verbatim during a smoke-test pass. After running the Teardown block exactly as written (copy-paste), $HOME/temp/.apra-fleet-tests still existed on disk with the full sandbox (.apra-fleet, .beads, .claude, .dolt, toy-repo, etc.) untouched, even though 'node dist/index.js stop' correctly stopped the server and the shell reported no error.
Repro:
1. In a fresh shell: export HOME=~/temp/.apra-fleet-tests (some path that does not yet exist is fine)
2. rm -rf ~/temp/.apra-fleet-tests
3. Observe this targets $HOME/temp/.apra-fleet-tests (i.e. .../temp/.apra-fleet-tests/temp/.apra-fleet-tests using the reassigned HOME), not the original real-$HOME/temp/.apra-fleet-tests -- confirm via 'echo ~/temp/.apra-fleet-tests' after the export to see the resolved path change.
Workaround used this run: deleted the sandbox with an explicit absolute path (/Users/<user>/temp/.apra-fleet-tests) instead of the tilde form, so the sandbox WAS actually removed before returning results -- but the playbook's own copy-paste block does not self-correct this, so every future run following it verbatim will silently accumulate/reuse a stale sandbox directory unless the runner notices independently.
Suggested fix direction (not applied -- do not modify integ-test-playbook.md per runner contract; a maintainer should update it): compute/store the absolute sandbox path in a variable BEFORE reassigning HOME (e.g. SANDBOX="$HOME/temp/.apra-fleet-tests" as the very first line, then 'rm -rf "$SANDBOX"' at the end), or use $USERPROFILE/$REAL_HOME captured pre-export, in both ## Teardown and anywhere else in the file that exports HOME and later re-uses a tilde path in the same block.
close_reason: Fix verified: current integ-test-playbook.md Teardown already uses the SANDBOX="$HOME/temp/.apra-fleet-tests" pattern (captured before HOME reassignment) as suggested. Re-ran Teardown verbatim this cycle (2026-07-20): rm -rf "$SANDBOX" correctly removed the sandbox (confirmed via ls exiting 'No such file or directory' afterward), no stale directory left behind.

title: [bug] execute_prompt busy-lock leaks: fleet_status idle but dispatch rejected with reason busy
description:
Observed twice on fleet server v0.3.5_8a1ef3 (log fleet-26772.log):
- 2026-07-19: fleet-dev held a busy lock ~20 min while idle (frozen token counts, no live process); during the apra-fleet-0ei hotfix track.
- 2026-07-20: two consecutive execute_prompt dispatches to fleet-dev returned {isError:true, reason:busy} while fleet_status reported the member idle (last session ~10h old).
Both times stop_prompt cleared it and reported 'no active session was running', proving the lock was stale, not a live prompt.

Likely cause: the in-flight dispatch lock (inFlightAgents or equivalent) is not released on some abandoned/killed dispatch path (client disconnect, process reap race).

Acceptance criteria:
- Lock release is tied to child-process reap AND client disconnect (no path leaves a lock with no live process).
- Add a TTL or liveness check: a busy rejection must verify the locked session has a live process; if not, self-heal (release + allow dispatch) and log a warning.
- fleet_status and the dispatch gate must agree: a member reported idle can never be rejected as busy.
- Regression test simulating an orphaned lock.

title: [test] deploy.md platform+arch selection routes darwin-x64 to source-build fallback
description:
Regression coverage for the eft.15 fix (guards against the fixed selection logic regressing). Since deploy.md is a runbook whose true end-to-end verification requires a Darwin x86_64 host, this test asserts the selection/fallback LOGIC statically from the runbook text so it is enforceable in CI on any platform.

FRAMEWORK/LOCATION: add a node --test file under packages/apra-fleet-workflow/test/ named e.g. deploy-runbook-platform-selection.test.mjs (matches the existing 'node --test test/*.test.mjs' suite). Read the repo-root deploy.md and parse its Deploy-section bash block.

WHAT TO ASSERT (each an independent test case):
1. Darwin + arm64 resolves to the prebuilt apra-fleet-installer-darwin-arm64 gh-run-download path.
2. Linux + x86_64 resolves to apra-fleet-installer-linux-x64.
3. Windows resolves to apra-fleet-installer-win-x64.exe.
4. Darwin + x86_64 does NOT attempt to download a (nonexistent) darwin-x64 artifact and instead reaches the build-from-source fallback (asserts presence of npm run build:binary in the fallback branch).
5. Every command invoked by the new fallback branch is covered by a matching prefix in deploy.md's Permissions section (and, if the test can read it, .claude/settings.json permissions.allow).

HOW TO ASSERT PASS/FAIL: prefer executing the extracted case/selection shell with a stubbed 'uname' (PATH-shadowed shell function) so the chosen ARTIFACT / fallback path is observable; if shell execution is impractical in the harness, fall back to structural assertions on the parsed bash block. Test must fail if the darwin-x64 fallback branch is removed.

DONE CRITERIA (acceptance): new .test.mjs passes under the package's npm test; it fails if deploy.md loses the darwin-x64 source-build fallback or a fallback command lacks a Permissions prefix; ASCII-only.
close_reason: Closed

title: [impl] Add architecture-aware build-from-source fallback to deploy.md Deploy section
description:
Fix eft.15 option (a): deploy.md's Deploy case statement selects an installer artifact from uname -s only, so a darwin-x64 (Intel Mac) host picks apra-fleet-installer-darwin-arm64 and install fails exit 127 'bad CPU type in executable' (CI publishes only win-x64, linux-x64, darwin-arm64).

FILES TO CHANGE:
- deploy.md (Deploy section bash block, Permissions section, Platform binaries table)
- .claude/settings.json (permissions.allow: add prefixes for any new commands)

BEHAVIOUR:
- Extend the artifact selection to branch on BOTH uname -s and uname -m. Combinations that HAVE a prebuilt CI artifact (Darwin+arm64 -> darwin-arm64; Linux+x86_64 -> linux-x64; Windows -> win-x64.exe) keep the existing gh-run-download + install --force path unchanged.
- For a host architecture with NO matching prebuilt artifact (notably Darwin+x86_64/Intel Mac), fall back to building the installer from source in the repo checkout: npm ci && npm run build && npm run build:binary, then run the produced installer binary with 'install --force'. Keep it self-contained on this branch (no main-branch CI change).
- Must be platform-agnostic (unchanged behaviour on win-x64, linux-x64, darwin-arm64) and idempotent across re-runs (clean work dir / --force).
- Add every new command (npm ci, npm run build, npm run build:binary, running the freshly built installer) to the deploy.md Permissions section prefixes AND .claude/settings.json permissions.allow.
- Note the source-build fallback in the Platform binaries table.

DONE CRITERIA (acceptance):
- deploy.md Deploy section contains an explicit no-matching-artifact fallback branch that build:binary-builds and installs from source, reached for Darwin+x86_64.
- Prebuilt-artifact platforms are unaffected.
- All new commands are covered by Permissions prefixes in both deploy.md and .claude/settings.json.
- File is ASCII-only (use - for dashes, -> for arrows, [OK] for checks).
- npm run build still succeeds; no non-ASCII introduced.
close_reason: Added architecture-aware (uname -s + uname -m) artifact selection to deploy.md's Deploy section with a source-build fallback (npm ci && npm run build && npm run build:binary, then run dist/apra-fleet-installer-<platform>-<arch> install --force) for OS+arch combos with no prebuilt CI artifact, notably Darwin+x86_64. Prebuilt platforms unaffected. Updated Permissions section and Platform binaries table in deploy.md, and permissions.allow in .claude/settings.json. File is ASCII-only; npm run build passes.

title: [bug] Deploy phase cannot run on darwin-x64 members: no matching installer artifact
description:
Observed run 12 Deploy C2 on fleet-rev (Darwin x86_64): deploy.md's platform selection correctly picked apra-fleet-installer-darwin-arm64 (the only macOS artifact CI publishes) but the install step failed with exit 127 'bad CPU type in executable' because this host is an Intel Mac. Main's CI matrix builds only win-x64, linux-x64, darwin-arm64.

Options considered:
(a) PREFERRED: add an architecture-aware fallback to deploy.md -- when no prebuilt artifact matches uname -s + uname -m, build the installer from source on this machine (npm ci, npm run build, npm run build:binary) and run the resulting installer with install --force. Self-contained on this branch, no main change needed.
(b) Add a darwin-x64 (macos-13 runner) entry to the CI build-binary matrix -- but the deploy pulls artifacts from MAIN's CI, so this only helps after a separate main-branch PR is merged; out of reach from this branch.
(c) Tarball flow (apra-fleet-tarball artifact + install.sh) -- currently NOT viable: the tarball omits install.cjs and scripts/ which install.sh requires, and the legacy tarball installer never creates ~/.apra-fleet/bin/apra-fleet, so deploy.md's smoke test can never pass. If chosen, fix the packaging first (main-branch change).

Acceptance criteria:
- deploy.md's Deploy section succeeds end-to-end on fleet-rev (Darwin x86_64): install completes and the smoke test exits 0.
- The mechanism is platform-agnostic (works unchanged on win-x64, linux-x64, darwin-arm64, darwin-x64) and idempotent across re-runs.
- Any new commands are covered by the Permissions section prefixes.
- ASCII only.
notes:
DESCOPED to P3 (convergence cut, 2026-07-21): no darwin-x64 member exists in the fleet (Mac members are arm64); speculative platform support. If deploy encounters an unmatched platform it should skip with a clear message -- fold that guard into any future revisit.
DESCOPE REVERTED (2026-07-21): the P3 demotion rationale was factually wrong -- fleet-rev itself is darwin-x64 (uname -m: x86_64, Intel Core i5-8257U), so the fleet's PRIMARY member is exactly the platform with no installer artifact. Back to P2, in gate. Fix direction: add darwin-x64 to the CI build-binary matrix (macos-13 GitHub runners are Intel; macos-latest is arm64) or cross-compile the SEA binary; deploy's platform->artifact mapping then resolves for fleet-rev.
FIX DIRECTION SIMPLIFIED (user, 2026-07-21): npm run build:binary is host-native and runs anywhere, including darwin-x64 -- no CI matrix growth needed. Deploy phase: when no prebuilt installer artifact matches the member's platform, fall back to building on the member itself (checkout branch, npm install && npm run build:binary, then run its installer). fleet-rev already builds this repo routinely, so the fallback is proven viable on the exact target. CI darwin-x64 artifact remains optional future polish, not the fix.
FACTS CORRECTED WITH EVIDENCE (2026-07-21): (1) GitHub public/free-plan runners do NOT offer Intel macOS -- CI/release builds darwin-arm64 ONLY; a darwin-x64 CI artifact is NOT an option (strike the earlier 'macos-13 Intel runners' claim). (2) fleet-rev (Intel Mac) ALREADY builds and runs the darwin-x64 binary natively -- verified on disk: dist/apra-fleet-installer-darwin-x64, Mach-O x86_64, v0.4.0_290661, built 2026-07-20 21:58 (the same build the run-16 integ sandbox executed). Fix therefore = deploy-phase fallback ONLY: no matching release artifact for the member's platform -> build on the member (npm run build:binary) and install that. Proven viable on the exact target.
STATUS AUDIT (2026-07-21): both children closed (15.1 arch-aware source-build fallback in deploy.md, 15.2 selection test) -- matches the agreed fix direction. No work remains; open pending integ verification (Deploy phase on fleet-rev/darwin-x64).
close_reason: integ-test-runner (2026-07-21): no longer reproduces. packages/apra-fleet-workflow/test/deploy-runbook-platform-selection.test.mjs (eft.15.2, 6/6 pass via 'node --test'): Darwin+x86_64 correctly skips the nonexistent darwin-x64 download artifact and takes the source-build fallback (npm run build:binary), which never invokes gh download; every fallback command carries a matching Permissions prefix.

title: Commit or discard the stray eft.13.4 working-tree changes on auto-sprint/eft-service
description: git status on auto-sprint/eft-service is dirty with uncommitted apra-fleet-eft.13.4 work not captured in any commit: tests/execute-prompt.test.ts (modified, adds the process-tree-kill-on-retry test) and packages/apra-fleet-workflow/test/apra-fleet-workflow-ephemeral-ports.test.mjs (untracked, part 1 of the eft.13.4 ephemeral-port regression coverage). Both pass in the suite but exist only in the working tree, so they would be lost if the branch is merged as-is. Commit them under the eft.13.4 bead (or remove them if superseded) so the branch's committed state matches the tree.
